title: WordPress Core "wp2shell" RCE flaws get public exploits, patch now
blurb: Public proof-of-concept exploits are now out for "wp2shell," a chain of two WordPress Core flaws (CVE-2026-63030 and CVE-2026-60137) that gives unauthenticated attackers remote code execution on a stock install with no plugins. WordPress has forced auto-updates for affected 6.9.x and 7.0.x sites and is telling admins to move to 7.0.2 or 6.9.5 immediately.

title: Qwen3.8 is launching and going open-weight soon
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen team said Qwen3.8 will launch soon and release as open weights, a 2.4T-parameter model the team positions as competitive with leading frontier systems and second only to Fable 5. A Qwen3.8-Max-Preview is already live for testing through Alibaba's Token Plan, Qoder, and QoderWork, with pricing available on Qwen's international and China cloud portals.

title: Claude Code uses Bun written in Rust now
blurb: Claude Code v2.1.181 and later ship the Rust port of Bun, which Jarred Sumner says made startup 10% faster on Linux with no other visible change. The author confirmed it locally by running strings on the Claude binary, which reports Bun v1.4.0 and lists 563 Rust source paths, ahead of any tagged Bun release outside canary.

title: Critical ServiceNow code execution flaw now exploited in attacks
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-6875, a pre-auth sandbox-escape RCE in ServiceNow AI Platform, days after the July 13th patch.

title: World's Largest AI Model Repository Hugging Face Breached by Autonomous AI Agent
blurb: Hugging Face said an autonomous AI agent framework breached its production infrastructure, using a malicious dataset to exploit two code execution paths and escalate to node-level access across internal clusters. The company found no evidence that public models, datasets, or Spaces were tampered with, and turned to Z.ai's GLM 5.2 for forensic analysis after Western frontier models refused to process the real attack payloads.

title: Xiaomi-Robotics-1
blurb: Xiaomi released Xiaomi-Robotics-1, a robot policy model pre-trained on 100,000 hours of embodiment-free UMI trajectories across more than 1,700 household, commercial, industrial, and outdoor scenarios. Post-training adds 7,200 hours of real-robot data collected in actual homes on tasks like tidying sofas and sorting shoe cabinets, testing whether robotics can follow the data-driven scaling curves that language and vision models ride.

title: Capital One Open Sources AI-Powered ‘VulnHunter’ Security Tool
blurb: Capital One open sourced VulnHunter, an agentic security tool that traces code paths to find exploitable defects and proposes targeted fixes rather than emitting scanner-style findings. The GitHub release includes a quickstart, architecture docs, and example workflows, but requires Claude Opus 4.8 and a working Claude Code environment, and Capital One says it ran across thousands of internal repositories.

title: Exploit brokers pay $500k for WordPress RCEs. I found one with GPT5.6 and $25
blurb: A Searchlight Cyber researcher found a pre-auth remote code execution chain in WordPress by adapting the prompt OpenAI published for GPT5.6 Sol Ultra's math work, running four agents for six hours at a total cost of $25. Similar bugs sell for $500,000 on the exploit broker market, and two other teams independently reproduced the full chain before public PoCs appeared on GitHub.

title: Moonshot AI’s Kimi K3 Stuns Investors, Challenges Anthropic, OpenAI Lead
blurb: Chinese lab Moonshot AI released Kimi K3, a model whose performance has investors reassessing how far ahead Anthropic and OpenAI actually are. The release adds to a pattern of Chinese labs closing the gap on frontier US models at lower cost, pressuring the assumption that compute scale and capital alone secure a durable lead.

title: Netflix paid $587M for Ben Affleck’s AI filmmaking startup
blurb: Netflix paid $587M in cash for InterPositive, the AI filmmaking startup co-founded by Ben Affleck, according to a new regulatory filing. The March acquisition brought the full team over with Affleck as a senior advisor, and its post-production tools fix missing shots, backgrounds, and lighting. Netflix said roughly 300 of its titles have already used generative AI.

title: The Kimi K3 Moment
blurb: The author argues that Kimi K3 matches Claude's top model on everyday coding work at a fraction of the price, with API rates of $3 and $15 per million input and output tokens against Claude's $10 and $50. He extends the point to US policy, noting that export and safety gating only constrains American customers while open Chinese models like GLM 5.2 ship under MIT licenses and take on work restricted models refuse.

title: Regressive JPEGs
blurb: Progressive JPEGs split image data into multiple scans so partially downloaded files render as a low-resolution preview, and the author found that concatenating several same-resolution images into one file makes later scans overwrite earlier ones, turning a slow connection into an animation. The trick caps out around nine frames because most decoders stop reading scans past that point as a zip-bomb defense.

title: On Flock License Plate Tracking Cameras
blurb: A viral account of a driver wrongly arrested traces the error to a partial license plate, 34 DTM, entered into Flock's system, which then flagged every Jaguar Land Rover media-fleet car sharing that plate structure. Flock said its matching worked as designed, since law enforcement asked for partial-plate hits, meaning one bad police report propagated nationwide across every department using the network.

title: Ernst & Young Data Breach Affects Personal, Financial Information
blurb: EY is notifying clients that a breached third-party tax support platform exposed names, Social Security numbers, and card data.

title: Moonshot’s Kimi K3 May Be More About Memory Than Compute
blurb: Moonshot AI released Kimi K3 on Friday, and semiconductor stocks fell sharply on renewed fears that frontier models need less compute than expected. The selloff echoes DeepSeek's R1 debut in early 2025, which erased roughly $600B from Nvidia's market value in a single day, though early reads suggest K3's gains come more from memory efficiency than raw training scale.

title: Jeff Bezos and Sovereign AI back CuspAI in $450M raise
blurb: Cambridge-based CuspAI raised $450M at a $2.6B valuation, a five-fold jump from its $520M mark last September. The Series B was co-led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA with participation from Bezos Expeditions and the UK's Sovereign AI Fund, and will fund an "AI Materials Foundry" network for designing materials for clean energy, semiconductors, and carbon capture alongside Nvidia, Meta, and Samsung.

title: Who’s Afraid of Chinese Models?
blurb: Ben Thompson argues that open weights models like China's Kimi K3 are not actually free, since downloading weights only skips R&D while COGS remains a real per-token cost that scales with usage. He frames this as marginal costs returning to the center of tech strategy after two decades in which zero-marginal-cost software drove the industry toward aggregation and scale.

title: What I learned selling 2,500 MIDI recorders: Hardware is not so hard
blurb: After selling 2,500 units of Jamcorder, a piano recording device, the author argues that hardware's reputation for difficulty is overstated and that software was by far the harder half. The firmware, app, and manufacturing tooling ran to roughly 200K lines over three years, while the hardware stayed simple by design with a single-screw assembly, one PCB, no power button, and no USB-C.

title: Transcribe.cpp
blurb: Transcribe.cpp is a new ggml-based speech-to-text library covering 16 ASR families and 60+ models, with GPU acceleration via Vulkan, Metal, CUDA, and TinyBLAS. The author built it for the cross-platform Handy app after finding whisper.cpp and ONNX too limiting, and every supported model has been numerically validated and WER tested against its reference implementation.

title: Setting up your spare Mac for Claude Code to control, a step-by-step guide
blurb: This guide walks through converting a spare Mac into a dedicated, always-on machine that Claude Code controls with computer use enabled, reachable from a phone via the Claude app or from a main Mac over SSH. The author argues a separate physical machine beats containerization for agents running with `--dangerously-skip-permissions`, since containers still route network traffic through the host and cannot run Mac-only apps like Unity.

title: Turso is building a modern version of Postgres in Rust
blurb: Turso announced it is building a Postgres-compatible database from scratch in Rust, layered on the same core that powers its SQLite rewrite. The design drops process-per-connection, runs asynchronously in browsers or embedded as a file, and supports MVCC concurrent writes plus materialized views that update themselves instead of requiring scheduled refreshes.

title: India's first privately-developed rocket reaches orbit on dramatic debut launch
blurb: Skyroot Aerospace's Vikram-1 reached a 280-mile orbit on its debut flight, making it India's first privately developed rocket to reach space. The 72-foot vehicle carries up to 770 pounds to low-Earth orbit, slightly more than Rocket Lab's Electron, and uses a 3D-printed engine on its liquid-fueled fourth stage.

title: Chinese open models like Kimi K3 and Qwen3.8 Max shift the industry's focus back to marginal costs and cost of goods sold, as inference costs scale with revenue (Ben Thompson/Stratechery)
blurb: Cheap Chinese open models like Kimi K3 and Qwen3.8 Max are pushing the industry's attention from training budgets back to marginal cost and cost of goods sold. The author argues that inference costs scale with revenue rather than sitting as a fixed capital expense, so AI products carry gross-margin math closer to hardware than to traditional software.

title: Cambridge-based CuspAI raised a $450M Series B led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA, and launches a coalition for AI materials discovery for chip and other industries (Bloomberg)
blurb: CuspAI raised a $450M Series B led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA to build AI models that design new materials from scratch. The Cambridge-based startup also launched a coalition aimed at applying materials discovery to chipmaking and other industrial sectors, where new compounds gate progress on cooling, packaging, and energy efficiency.

title: Hugging Face experienced cyberattack carried out end-to-end by agentic AI
blurb: Hugging Face disclosed a breach in which the entire attack chain, from reconnaissance through exfiltration, was executed by an agentic AI system rather than a human operator. The company has since secured its systems, contacted law enforcement, and asked registered users to rotate access tokens and review account activity.

title: Alibaba Says New AI Model Is Just Second to Anthropic’s Fable 5
blurb: Alibaba previewed a new AI model it says ranks second only to Anthropic's Fable 5 on benchmark performance. The claim adds to evidence that Chinese labs are closing the gap with US frontier models, though Alibaba has not yet published full evaluation results or a release date.

title: Alibaba previews Qwen3.8, claims it’s second only to Claude Fable 5
blurb: Alibaba previewed Qwen3.8-Max-Preview at the World AI Conference in Shanghai, a 2.4T-parameter multimodal model the company claims ranks second only to Anthropic's Claude Fable 5. The claim shipped without benchmark scores, a model card, or activated-parameter counts, unlike Qwen3.7-Max in May. It is available now at 10% of standard pricing through Alibaba's Token Plan and Qoder platforms.

title: TuxBot v3 Evolution: Inside an IoT Botnet Framework With LLM-Assisted Development
blurb: Unit 42 documented TuxBot v3, a modular IoT botnet whose LLM-written code shipped with the AI's safety disclaimer still in it.

title: Sources: parts of the Trump administration are reigniting efforts to implement de facto bans on foreign open-source models, as Chinese AI models gain momentum (Maria Curi/Axios)
blurb: Parts of the Trump administration are reviving efforts to impose de facto bans on foreign open-source AI models, according to sources cited by Axios. The push follows growing adoption of Chinese open-weight models among US developers, and would target distribution rather than the models themselves, raising questions about how far restrictions could reach into repositories like Hugging Face.

title: Sources: Chinese startup Moonshot AI is seeking formal approval from investors to start a Hong Kong IPO process, which could happen in as soon as six months (Juro Osawa/The Information)
blurb: Moonshot AI is seeking investor approval to begin a Hong Kong IPO process that could start in as soon as six months, according to The Information. The Chinese startup behind the Kimi models would be among the first of China's new AI labs to test public markets, at a moment when domestic frontier labs are under pressure to show revenue rather than benchmark wins.

title: TSMC’s $265 Billion US Spend Driven by Demand, Rivals, CFO Says
blurb: TSMC is adding $100B to its Arizona buildout, bringing total planned US investment to $265B. The company's CFO attributed the expansion to demand from American customers and pressure from competitors moving into advanced chipmaking, positioning the Arizona fabs as a hedge against both supply-chain concentration in Taiwan and rivals like Samsung and Intel courting the same clients.

title: Biggest Probabilistic Computer Turns Noise into Answers
blurb: Researchers built a probabilistic computer with 1 million p-bits, up from 7,200 in 2023, by networking multiple FPGAs instead of confining the machine to a single chip. P-bits flip between 0 and 1 at a tunable probability and solve optimization problems through correlated noise, offering a programmable alternative to quantum computing and to fixed-function Ising machines without the cryogenic hardware demands.

title: Our Approach to Bioresilience: Isomorphic Labs and Google DeepMind
blurb: Google DeepMind and Isomorphic Labs published a joint bioresilience approach covering how they restrict misuse of their models and how they open them to vetted biosecurity partners. The program spans more than 15 partnerships built over the past 12 months, and includes work to adapt SynthID watermarking to biology so DNA synthesis providers can screen for AI-generated sequences.

title: The EU fines Alibaba's online marketplace AliExpress €550M for failing to do enough to prevent the sale of illegal products, the largest DSA fine to date (Barbara Moens/Financial Times)
blurb: The EU fined AliExpress €550M for failing to adequately prevent the sale of illegal products on its marketplace, the largest penalty issued under the Digital Services Act to date. The scale of the fine sets a benchmark for how aggressively Brussels intends to enforce DSA obligations against large online platforms, particularly foreign-owned marketplaces handling third-party sellers.

title: Moonshot’s Kimi AI Model Sets Off Anxiety in the US
blurb: Chinese startup Moonshot AI released Kimi K3, reigniting debate in the US over how far domestic labs lead on frontier models. The release lands as open-weight Chinese models keep closing the gap on Western benchmarks at a fraction of the training cost, pressuring US labs on both pricing and release cadence.

title: From $520M to $2.6B in 9 months: Bezos Expeditions co-leads CuspAI’s $450M Series B to scale AI materials discovery
blurb: CuspAI raised $450M in a Series B at a $2.6B valuation, led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA with participation from Bezos Expeditions. The Cambridge, UK startup's valuation rose from $520M nine months ago, and its MIRA platform, which suggests chemical compositions matching requested physical properties, is already used by ASML, Meta, Samsung, and Hyundai.

title: CuspAI lands $450m round to accelerate AI materials discovery
blurb: CuspAI raised a $450M Series B at a $2.6B valuation, led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA with participation from Bezos Expeditions. The Cambridge-based startup, founded in 2024 by Chad Edwards and Max Welling, also launched an AI Materials Foundry with more than 45 founding members including Nvidia, Meta's FAIR team, Samsung, and Merck, quadrupling its valuation since a $100M round last September.

title: Alibaba’s Qwen Unveils Preview of Flagship AI Model
blurb: Alibaba released a preview of Qwen3.8 Max, its new flagship model, and positioned it as second only to Anthropic's Fable 5 among frontier systems. The preview release gives Alibaba a claim to the top tier of Chinese models while it courts enterprise cloud customers weighing US alternatives.

title: AliExpress fined almost $630 million over illegal product sales
blurb: The European Commission fined AliExpress €550M (about $629M) for failing to stop the sale of counterfeit clothing, unsafe toys, and dangerous cosmetics on its platform. It is the largest penalty yet under the Digital Services Act, ahead of the $230M levied against Temu in May, and AliExpress has until October 20, 2026 to fix the breach or face additional periodic fines.

title: China delivers a one-two punch to America’s AI dominance
blurb: Moonshot released Kimi K3, a 2.8T-parameter model it calls the largest open-source AI system, and Alibaba followed days later with a preview of the 2.4T-parameter Qwen3.8. Both companies claim performance near OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol and Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 at lower cost, and both plan to publish weights, with Moonshot's release set for July 27th.

title: Prysmian Signs $6.3 Billion Molex Deal for Data-Center Gear
blurb: Prysmian signed a deal worth up to €5.5B ($6.3B) to supply optical cables to Koch Inc.'s Molex for its data centers. The agreement locks in long-term demand for Prysmian's fiber capacity as AI buildouts push cabling and interconnect suppliers into the same supply crunch already hitting chips and power equipment.

title: What to Know About the Chinese AI Models Rattling U.S. Stocks
blurb: Chinese labs including Moonshot are shipping frontier-class models like Kimi K3 at a fraction of US training costs, pressuring American AI stocks. The concern is that cheap, capable open-weight competition undercuts the capex assumptions behind the US buildout, where hundreds of billions in chip and data center spending depends on frontier models staying expensive to build.

title: Politicians Are Trying to Change What Chatbots Say About Them
blurb: Political campaigns are now monitoring what chatbots tell voters about their candidates, treating AI answers as a reputation surface alongside search results and press coverage. Staffers have little recourse beyond publishing more favorable content and hoping models pick it up, since there is no equivalent of SEO tooling or a correction process for model outputs.

title: Earth-like LHS 1140b May Feature the First Atmosphere Found on Exoplanet
blurb: A paper in Science reports time-variable helium escape from LHS 1140b, a super-Earth 49 light-years away, suggesting a helium-rich upper atmosphere depleted in hydrogen. If confirmed, it would be the first atmosphere detected on a potentially habitable rocky exoplanet, though the planet orbits a red dwarf every 25 days and receives less than half the light Earth gets from the Sun.

title: Space Force triples launch contract ceiling amid rising demand
blurb: The Space Force raised the ceiling on its National Security Space Launch Phase 3 Lane 1 contract from $5.6B to $17B, expanding what it can spend on task orders through fiscal 2029. Seven providers compete for those missions, including SpaceX, ULA, Blue Origin, Rocket Lab, Stoke Space, Impulse Space and Relativity Space, and the military has already identified 25 Lane 2 launches beyond the 54 originally planned.

title: Skyroot Aerospace reaches orbit on first Vikram-1 launch
blurb: Skyroot Aerospace's Vikram-1 reached a 450-kilometer low Earth orbit on its first flight, making it the first commercial Indian rocket to do so. The three-stage solid-fuel vehicle carried two cubesats and can lift 350 kilograms to low Earth orbit, with an upgraded Vikram-1U planned at 550 kilograms after a series of test flights.

title: Google preparing Gemini Live and Skills for web rollout
blurb: Recent Gemini web builds contain a Gemini Live button and traces of skills being wired into regular chats, suggesting Google will bring its real-time voice mode and reusable instruction packages beyond mobile and its Ultra-tier Spark agent. Opening skills to ordinary chat users would close a visible gap with Claude and ChatGPT, though nothing has been announced and Gemini 3.5 Pro has already slipped past a mid-July target.

title: Chinese solar company says its new cell has an efficiency of 35.5 percent
blurb: LONGi says its crystalline silicon-perovskite tandem solar cell hit 35.5% conversion efficiency, verified by the European Solar Test Installation. Commercial panels are only now approaching 25%, though the NREL built a 39.5% cell back in 2022, and the open question for tandem cells remains mass production rather than lab records.

title: Memory chip boss admits RAM prices are 'abnormally high' — SK Group chairman considering building a semiconductor plant in the US to expand supply, calm ‘chipflation’
blurb: SK Group chairman Chey Tae-won said memory prices are "abnormally high" and that the industry needs to expand supply, and he is weighing a memory chip plant in the US. His concern is that PC and smartphone makers pass DRAM costs to consumers, and that sustained high margins invite new entrants like China's CXMT and YMTC, which Corsair, Lenovo, and Apple have already approached for supply.

title: Odd Lots: How Claude Code Is Reshaping Software (Podcast)
blurb: Bloomberg's Odd Lots podcast covers how Claude Code and similar agentic coding tools are changing the economics of software development. The discussion focuses on what shifts when writing code stops being the bottleneck, including the effect on engineering headcount, the widening gap between prototype and production, and whether incumbent software vendors keep their pricing power.

title: Clever hacker fits 537,000 domains in a tiny $5 ESP32 ad-blocking dongle — firmware uses only around 50KB of RAM and can answer blocked lookups in 10 milliseconds
blurb: A $5 ESP32-C3 stores 537,000 blocked domains as sorted 40-bit hashes, answering DNS lookups in 10ms on 50KB of RAM.

title: Bezos backs CuspAI as startup teams up with Nvidia to hunt for chipmaking materials
blurb: CuspAI raised $450M at a $2.6B valuation, led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA with participation from Jeff Bezos' Bezos Expeditions, AMD Ventures, and Lux Capital. The British startup uses AI to simulate novel materials before lab testing, and launched an "AI Materials Foundry" with Nvidia, Meta's FAIR team, and 43 other organizations supplying compute and chemistry expertise.

title: The Massive Supply Deals Feeding the AI Frenzy Are No Sure Thing
blurb: The multiyear supply agreements that memory and component makers point to as proof of durable AI demand often carry weaker commitments than the headlines suggest. Deals touted by suppliers like SK Hynix frequently lack binding volume or price terms, leaving revenue exposed if buyers scale back orders as capacity catches up with demand.

title: The EU just fined AliExpress €550M, its biggest DSA penalty yet
blurb: The European Commission fined AliExpress €550M for failing to keep counterfeit clothing, unsafe toys, and dangerous cosmetics off its platform, the largest penalty yet under the Digital Services Act. It is the third DSA fine after X's €120M in December and Temu's €200M in May, and the Commission said it went easier than it could have because the law is still new.

title: Hardcore IndieWeb: Run your own website 100% independently for only $0.01/day
blurb: A guide to hosting a fully self-owned site with local-first files and static HTML, for about $0.01/day.

title: Co-evolution of self-replication and function in a digital primordial soup
blurb: Researchers seeded a population of random 32-byte Z80 assembly programs and let self-replication emerge from mutation alone, then tied reproduction odds to correctly evaluating a polynomial. Self-replication and math ability co-evolved, with task pressure producing more compact replicators and spatial task partitioning generating an emergent curriculum where simple solutions became stepping stones to harder ones.

title: Engineering High-Performance Parsers with Data-Oriented Design
blurb: Yuku, a JavaScript/TypeScript parser written in Zig, runs 3-10x faster than npm alternatives by storing the AST as flat arrays instead of a pointer tree. The author replaces every node pointer with a u32 index into one array, cutting 50,000 allocations per 100 KB file down to a handful and turning tree traversal into a linear scan the prefetcher can follow.

title: Netflix kauft für fast 600 Millionen Ben Afflecks KI-Startup
blurb: Netflix paid $587M in cash for InterPositive, the AI post-production startup co-founded by Ben Affleck. The technology fills in missing shots, swaps backgrounds, and corrects lighting errors, and the full team moves to Netflix with Affleck staying on as a senior advisor. Roughly 300 Netflix films and series now use generative AI somewhere in production or post.

title: Better and Cheaper Than IPTV
blurb: Castor extracts the real video stream from a web page via headless Chrome, transcodes it, and casts to your TV from the terminal.

title: Towards a Harness That Can Do Anything
blurb: The author argues that a good agent harness should minimize the tokens spent on cognitive load, keeping the core prompt small and letting the model load skills into context at runtime. The post's central claim is that harnesses should lean on what LLMs already know well, coding and systems administration, rather than teaching them novel environments, while handling logging, sanity checks, and failsafes invisibly in the background.

title: When can a power company take your land for data center infrastructure?
blurb: Power companies in states including Georgia and Pennsylvania are invoking eminent domain to run transmission lines across private land for data centers, and a legal scholar argues the cases hinge on whether infrastructure serving a private facility counts as "public use" under the Fifth Amendment. Data centers consumed over 4% of US electricity in 2024, and with 1,500 more in development against 7 in 10 Americans opposing local construction, these takings fights are likely to multiply.

title: A new Intel Itanium (IA-64) emulator that boots Windows
blurb: Yufeng Gao released version 0.1 of an Intel Itanium (IA-64) emulator that boots Windows Server 2003 and Windows XP 64-bit. Performance sits around 486 levels on a Ryzen 5000 chip, and OpenVMS, HP-UX, Linux, and BSD do not boot yet, but the project is the first known Itanium emulator of any kind, with source planned for release once cleaned up.

title: UnifiedIR for Julia
blurb: A Julia core developer opened an RFC to replace the language's decade-old compiler IR data structures with UnifiedIR, a single dialect-aware, region-based representation similar to MLIR. It would consolidate SyntaxTree, SyntaxGraph, CodeInfo, and IRCode into one extensible package that ecosystem projects can build on instead of maintaining their own IRs, with layered views for compaction passes, CFG transforms, and lowering.

title: Alibaba Fined Record €550 Million by EU for Illegal Sales
blurb: The EU fined Alibaba's e-commerce arm €550M ($629M) for selling illegal products on its platform, the largest penalty issued under the Digital Services Act to date. The size of the fine sets a new ceiling for DSA enforcement and signals that regulators will treat marketplace listings with the same scrutiny they apply to content moderation on social platforms.

title: The Creator of Claude Code on the Hottest Piece of Software in the World
blurb: Boris Cherny, the creator of Claude Code, discussed how coding agents have changed the day-to-day work of software engineers in a Bloomberg interview. He argues the job is shifting from writing code to specifying and reviewing it, with engineers supervising multiple agent sessions rather than typing every line themselves.

title: Google has to play fair with AI rivals on Android, and that could be good news for your wallet
blurb: The European Commission ordered Google on July 16 to give rival AI apps the same deep Android access Gemini gets, including voice-trigger wake words, cross-app task execution, sensor context, and on-device models. Google has until August 1, 2027 to comply and has warned the changes create privacy and security risks, while Apple took the opposite path and confirmed Siri AI will ship without EU availability in iOS 27.

title: Samsung cuts hundreds of US consumer electronics jobs ahead of Texas HQ move — 739 roles affected in New Jersey as chip division posts record profit
blurb: Samsung filed a WARN notice covering 739 roles at its Englewood Cliffs, New Jersey campus as it moves Samsung Electronics America's headquarters to Texas by year-end, with roughly 100 more cut in Plano. The cuts hit phones, TVs, and appliances while Samsung's chip division posted a preliminary $58.5B in Q2 operating profit, a 19-fold jump that topped Nvidia's latest quarter.

title: What to watch for after Jensen Huang’s Japan visit
blurb: Jensen Huang's two-day Tokyo visit produced deals across Japan's industrial stack, including a Vera Rubin AI factory with 13,750 CPUs and 27,500 GPUs due in 2028. Japan is spending up to 1 trillion yen ($6.2B) over five years on Noetra, a 44-firm sovereign AI effort for robots and factories, while Fanuc, Yaskawa, Kawasaki Heavy and others commit to Nvidia's Cosmos models.

title: NYC may require landlords and realtors to disclose the use of AI in listings
blurb: New York City's "Rental Ripoff Report" recommends requiring landlords and realtors to disclose when rental listings have been altered with AI, including generated or edited photos. The proposal sits alongside measures recognizing tenant unions and expanding bargaining rights, and would make NYC one of the first jurisdictions to treat undisclosed AI imagery as a deceptive advertising practice.

title: TSMC is accelerating Arizona factory buildout to capitalize on AI 'megatrend,' CFO says
blurb: TSMC committed an additional $100B to its Arizona buildout, raising total planned investment there to $265B.

title: Prysmian signs €5.5bn fibre deal with Molex to wire AI data centres
blurb: Prysmian signed a €5.5bn, 10-year deal with Molex to supply optical cable for AI data centre interiors, with €550m paid upfront.

title: A new AI model wants self-driving cars to think before they swerve
blurb: Researchers at Seoul National University released SafeDrive, an end-to-end autonomous driving model that generates multiple candidate trajectories, scores each for safety against sensor data, and picks the best one instead of committing to a single predicted path. The work was selected as a CVPR 2026 highlight paper and has been folded into EAD, a reference model backed by Korea's Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy, with road testing underway at domestic AV companies.

title: AMD acquires FastFlowLM to accelerate on-device AI inferencing
blurb: AMD acquired FastFlowLM, the team behind an open-source runtime for running AI models locally on AMD neural processing units. The group joins AMD's AI Group to speed up its client and workstation AI software stack, extending the company's push to counter Nvidia's proprietary tooling with open alternatives like ROCm. Terms were not disclosed.

title: Qwen3.8 is launching and going open-weight soon
blurb: Alibaba announced that Qwen3.8, a 2.4T-parameter model, will launch soon and ship with open weights. A Qwen3.8-Max-Preview is already accessible through Alibaba's Token Plan, Qoder, and QoderWork, and the company positions the model as competitive with leading frontier systems, claiming it trails only Fable 5.

title: Claude Code uses Bun written in Rust now
blurb: Claude Code v2.1.181 and later ship the Rust port of Bun, per Bun creator Jarred Sumner, and Simon Willison confirmed it by running `strings` against his own install. The binary reports Bun v1.4.0, ahead of the latest tagged release, and contains 563 `.rs` source paths. Startup got 10% faster on Linux, with no other user-visible change.

title: Capital One Open Sources AI-Powered ‘VulnHunter’ Security Tool
blurb: Capital One open sourced VulnHunter, an agentic security tool that identifies exploitable defects, maps attack paths, and proposes targeted code remediations rather than just flagging issues. The GitHub release includes a quickstart guide, architecture documentation, and example workflows, and currently requires Claude Opus 4.8 and a working Claude Code environment to run.

title: World's Largest AI Model Repository Hugging Face Breached by Autonomous AI Agent
blurb: Hugging Face said an autonomous AI agent breached its production infrastructure, gaining access to internal datasets and service credentials after a malicious dataset exploited two code execution paths in its data processing pipeline. The agent framework ran thousands of actions across short-lived sandboxes, and Hugging Face used Z.ai's GLM 5.2 for forensic analysis after Western frontier models refused to process the real attack payloads.

title: Exploit brokers pay $500k for WordPress RCEs. I found one with GPT5.6 and $25
blurb: A security researcher at Searchlight Cyber found a pre-authentication RCE chain in WordPress using GPT5.6 Sol Ultra and about $25 of compute. The prompt was adapted from the one OpenAI published for the model's Cycle Double Cover conjecture solution, directing four parallel agents to analyze the codebase from first principles for at least six hours without consulting changelogs or git history.

title: Hugging Face Breached Through a Malicious Dataset: What the Patches Reveal
blurb: Hugging Face disclosed a July 16 compromise of part of its production infrastructure that began with a malicious dataset abusing a remote-code loader and template injection in a dataset configuration, then escalated to node-level access and lateral movement across internal clusters. The author reconstructs the response from public dataset-viewer commits, which show an fsspec allowlist, ServiceAccount token mounting disabled on worker pods, and a shift to IRSA that removed static S3 and MongoDB credentials.

title: Russian-Speaking Hacker Uses Google Gemini CLI to Control Botnet of Eight Dental Clinic PCs
blurb: Trend Micro analyzed 200 Gemini CLI session logs from a solo Russian-speaking threat actor called "bandcampro," who used the agent to migrate command-and-control infrastructure and run a botnet of eight dental clinic computers. The actor bypassed guardrails by posing as an authorized pentester, and the entire C&C operation fit in three plaintext files totaling roughly 5 KB.

title: Netflix paid $587M for Ben Affleck’s AI filmmaking startup
blurb: Netflix paid $587M in cash for InterPositive, the AI filmmaking startup co-founded by Ben Affleck, according to a new regulatory filing. The company acquired InterPositive in March without disclosing terms, and its post-production tools address missing shots, background replacements, and incorrect lighting. Netflix said around 300 of its titles have already used generative AI.

title: Xiaomi-Robotics-1
blurb: Xiaomi released Xiaomi-Robotics-1, a robot policy model pre-trained on 100,000 hours of embodiment-free UMI trajectories spanning more than 1,700 scenarios. Post-training adds over 7,200 hours of in-house real-robot data collected in homes on tasks like tidying sofas and sorting shoe cabinets, with an automated pipeline labeling pre-training clips using a vision-language model.

title: Why goodput matters more than throughput for LLM serving
blurb: This post argues that throughput is the wrong primary metric for LLM serving, since requests per second can climb while time to first token and time per output token blow past latency targets. The author benchmarks goodput, the rate of completed requests that also meet those targets, using vLLM serving Qwen2.5-7B on a single NVIDIA A10G in EKS, with GuideLLM driving load and Prometheus, Grafana, and DCGM capturing metrics. The Kubernetes manifests are on GitHub.

title: The Kimi K3 Moment
blurb: The author argues that Kimi K3 matches Claude on everyday coding work at similar token counts, while costing $3 per million input and $15 per million output tokens against Claude's $10 and $50. The subscription gap is wider, with Kimi's coding tier at $39 a month. The post extends this into a policy critique, noting that Semgrep found GLM 5.2 outperforming Claude on cyber benchmarks because the restricted model declines the work and the open one does not.

title: Moonshot AI’s Kimi K3 Stuns Investors, Challenges Anthropic, OpenAI Lead
blurb: Moonshot AI released Kimi K3, a model that Bloomberg reports has drawn strong investor attention and put pressure on the assumption that Anthropic and OpenAI hold a durable frontier lead. The release adds to a pattern of Chinese labs closing the capability gap at lower cost, which complicates US pricing power in the API and enterprise markets.

title: Moonshot’s Kimi K3 May Be More About Memory Than Compute
blurb: Moonshot AI released Kimi K3 on Friday, and semiconductor stocks fell sharply on the view that the model shifts the bottleneck from compute toward memory. The reaction echoed DeepSeek's R1 debut in early 2025, which erased roughly $600B from Nvidia's market value in a single day on fears that AI would need less computing power than expected.

title: Jeff Bezos and Sovereign AI back CuspAI in $450M raise
blurb: Cambridge-based CuspAI raised $450M in a Series B co-led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA at a $2.6B valuation, a five-fold jump from its $520M mark last September. The startup's platform lets customers including ASML and Meta specify material properties they need, and the funding will support an "AI Materials Foundry" network with Nvidia, Samsung, Hyundai, and 40 other partners.

title: Who’s Afraid of Chinese Models?
blurb: Ben Thompson argues that open weights models like Kimi K3 aren't actually free, because the "free" only applies to R&D, while COGS remains real and rising for anyone serving inference. As Chinese open models approach state-of-the-art capabilities, marginal costs return as the defining constraint on AI industry structure, reversing the zero-marginal-cost dynamics that shaped the internet era and produced Aggregation Theory.

title: Cambridge-based CuspAI raised a $450M Series B led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA, and launches a coalition for AI materials discovery for chip and other industries (Bloomberg)
blurb: CuspAI raised a $450M Series B led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA to expand its AI system for discovering new materials. The Cambridge-based startup also launched a coalition aimed at applying materials discovery to chipmaking and other industrial sectors.

title: Transcribe.cpp
blurb: transcribe.cpp is a ggml-based speech-to-text library from Handy's maintainer that supports 16 ASR families and over 60 models, with GPU acceleration via Vulkan, Metal, CUDA, and TinyBLAS. Every supported model has been numerically validated and WER tested against its reference implementation, and the v0.1.0 release works as a near drop-in whisper.cpp replacement with streaming, batch transcription, and maintainer-supported bindings.

title: Setting up your spare Mac for Claude Code to control, a step-by-step guide
blurb: This guide walks through configuring a spare Mac as a dedicated always-on machine for Claude Code, with computer use enabled and access from an iPhone via the Claude app or from a main Mac over SSH. The author's argument for hardware isolation over containers is that containers still route network traffic through the host and cannot run Mac-only apps like Unity, which matters when running with `--dangerously-skip-permissions`.

title: Chinese open models like Kimi K3 and Qwen3.8 Max shift the industry's focus back to marginal costs and cost of goods sold, as inference costs scale with revenue (Ben Thompson/Stratechery)
blurb: Ben Thompson argues that cheap Chinese open models like Kimi K3 and Qwen3.8 Max are pushing AI economics back toward marginal costs and cost of goods sold. Unlike traditional software, where distribution is nearly free, inference costs scale with revenue, so every additional user carries real compute expense. The implication is that AI companies will be valued on gross margins rather than the near-infinite-leverage assumptions applied to SaaS.

title: A forensic tool for backdoored code completions in AI assistants
blurb: Researchers at the University of Louisville and the University of North Texas built CodeTracer, a forensic system that traces a model's harmful code completion back to the poisoned training examples that caused it. It works without gradients or training logs, summarizing the unsafe behavior, retrieving semantically similar training code with UniXcoder, then using GPT-4.1 to judge the top 500 candidates.

title: Germany's Soofi S AI Model Tops All Open-Source Rivals on German Benchmarks
blurb: A German consortium coordinated by the KI Bundesverband released Soofi S, an open-source German-English foundation model that scored 79.1 on the German aggregate, ahead of every other fully open model tested. The 30B hybrid Mamba-MoE model activates about 3.2B parameters per token and trained on Deutsche Telekom's Munich data center using roughly 253,000 B200 GPU-hours, though Qwen3.5 still leads on English and reasoning benchmarks.

title: Claude Fable produced a counterexample to the Jacobian Conjecture
blurb: Mathematician Levent Alpöge shared a polynomial map from C^3 to C^3, produced with Anthropic's Claude Fable, that he says refutes the Jacobian Conjecture. The map has constant Jacobian determinant -2 but sends three distinct points to (-1/4, 0, 0), contradicting the conjecture's claim that a polynomial map with nonvanishing constant Jacobian determinant must be invertible.

title: Hugging Face experienced cyberattack carried out end-to-end by agentic AI
blurb: Hugging Face disclosed a breach in which the entire attack chain, from reconnaissance through exfiltration, was executed by agentic AI rather than a human operator. The company has since secured its systems and contacted law enforcement, and is asking registered users to rotate access tokens and review account activity.

title: Fable 5 vs. GPT-5.6 Sol on an NP-Hard Problem: Does /goal help?
blurb: The author benchmarked Claude Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol on KIRO, an unpublished NP-hard fiber-network design problem with a search space around 10^1223 for the Paris instance. Fable 5 produced the best solution overall and was far more consistent across runs. Each model's native /goal mode changed the search path rather than reliably improving it, sometimes finding a better basin and sometimes giving a bad idea more time to mature.

title: TuxBot v3 Evolution: Inside an IoT Botnet Framework With LLM-Assisted Development
blurb: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 documented TuxBot v3 Evolution, a modular IoT botnet framework whose authors used an LLM to help write the code and shipped it with the model's safety disclaimer still in the source. The framework cross-compiles for 17 architectures, brute-forces Telnet with 1,496 credential pairs, and carries exploits for more than 30 IoT device families, though several LLM-generated functions failed to work in the analyzed samples.

title: Alibaba previews Qwen3.8, claims it’s second only to Claude Fable 5
blurb: Alibaba previewed Qwen3.8-Max-Preview at the World AI Conference in Shanghai, a 2.4T-parameter model and the first Qwen above 1T parameters to handle images, video, and documents alongside text. The Qwen team claimed it ranks second only to Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 but published no benchmark scores, model card, or activated-parameter count, a break from Qwen3.7-Max, which shipped in May with full results.

title: Sources: parts of the Trump administration are reigniting efforts to implement de facto bans on foreign open-source models, as Chinese AI models gain momentum (Maria Curi/Axios)
blurb: Parts of the Trump administration are reviving efforts to impose de facto bans on foreign open-source AI models, according to sources cited by Axios. The push follows growing adoption of Chinese open-weight models among US developers, and would likely restrict downloads or commercial use rather than target the models directly.

title: Sources: Chinese startup Moonshot AI is seeking formal approval from investors to start a Hong Kong IPO process, which could happen in as soon as six months (Juro Osawa/The Information)
blurb: Moonshot AI is seeking formal approval from its investors to begin a Hong Kong IPO process, according to sources cited by The Information. The listing could come in as soon as six months, which would make the Kimi developer one of the first Chinese frontier labs to go public.

title: Alibaba Says New AI Model Is Just Second to Anthropic’s Fable 5
blurb: Alibaba previewed a new AI model that it says trails only Anthropic's Fable 5 among frontier systems. The claim is the latest datapoint in China's narrowing capability gap with US labs, though Alibaba has not yet released independent benchmark results to support the second-place positioning.

title: Databricks raises at $188B as Coatue’s enterprise AI governance play outpaces the models underneath it
blurb: Databricks signed a term sheet for a strategic round at a $188B valuation led by Coatue Management, up 40% from $134B in February and expected to close later this summer. The capital goes toward Unity AI Gateway, Genie, and Lakebase, the governance and agent-data layer that generated $1.4B of the company's $5.4B annualized run rate.

title: Moonshot’s Kimi AI Model Sets Off Anxiety in the US
blurb: Moonshot AI released Kimi K3, its latest frontier model, prompting renewed debate in the US over how quickly Chinese labs are closing the capability gap. The reaction follows a pattern set by earlier open-weight Chinese releases, where competitive benchmark results at lower training cost reopened questions about export controls and the durability of US model leadership.

title: CuspAI lands $450m round to accelerate AI materials discovery
blurb: CuspAI raised $450M at a $2.6B valuation in a round led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA, with participation from Bezos Expeditions, AMD Ventures, and Britain's Sovereign AI Venture Fund. The Cambridge-based company also launched its AI Materials Foundry, a network of more than 45 founding members including Nvidia, Meta's FAIR team, Samsung, and Merck that pools compute, labs, and proprietary data for materials discovery.

title: LoRA Speedrun – a public wall-clock leaderboard for fine-tuning techniques
blurb: LoRA Speedrun is a public leaderboard for parameter-efficient fine-tuning that fixes the task, the hardware, and the caps, then ranks submissions by wall-clock time. Two frozen tracks run on a single Modal L40S, and every record is re-run three times with fresh seeds before it counts. The current top entry hits ≥57% on GSM8K with Qwen2.5-1.5B in 6m 05s using sequence packing and completion-only loss masking.

title: Alibaba’s Qwen Unveils Preview of Flagship AI Model
blurb: Alibaba released a preview of Qwen3.8 Max, its new flagship model, which the company positioned as comparable to leading frontier systems and second only to Anthropic's Fable 5. The preview release gives Alibaba a benchmark claim against US labs while it continues training ahead of a general availability launch.

title: From $520M to $2.6B in 9 months: Bezos Expeditions co-leads CuspAI’s $450M Series B to scale AI materials discovery
blurb: CuspAI raised $450M in a Series B at a $2.6B valuation, led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA with participation from Bezos Expeditions. The Cambridge, UK company's valuation has risen almost fivefold from $520M in nine months, and its MIRA materials search engine is already used by ASML, Meta, Samsung, and Hyundai.

title: Chinese President Xi Jinping wants emergency response systems to keep AI in check
blurb: Chinese President Xi Jinping called for emergency response systems to manage AI risks, framing oversight as a state responsibility rather than an industry one. The remarks push Beijing toward mandatory incident-reporting and shutdown mechanisms for advanced models, extending a regulatory posture that already includes algorithm registration and generative AI content rules.

title: What to Know About the Chinese AI Models Rattling U.S. Stocks
blurb: Chinese labs including Moonshot have released models such as Kimi K3 that approach frontier US performance at lower cost, pressuring US tech stocks. The concern for investors is that cheap, capable open-weight competitors undercut the spending assumptions behind the current AI capex boom.

title: Moonshot Is Creating New Winners and Losers in the AI Trade
blurb: Moonshot AI's surprise release of a new model moved markets, forcing investors to reconsider core assumptions behind the global tech rally. The reaction split the AI trade, with capital rotating away from names whose valuations depend on frontier labs holding a durable lead over cheaper Chinese competitors.

title: China delivers a one-two punch to America’s AI dominance
blurb: Moonshot released Kimi K3, a 2.8T-parameter model it calls the largest open-source AI system, and Alibaba followed within days with a preview of Qwen3.8 at 2.4T parameters. Both companies claim performance close to GPT-5.6 Sol and Claude Fable 5, and both plan open weights, with Moonshot's full release set for July 27th.

title: Google preparing Gemini Live and Skills for web rollout
blurb: Recent Gemini web builds contain a Gemini Live button and traces of skills being wired into regular chats, signaling that both features may move beyond their current limits. Live is currently mobile-only and in Trusted Tester testing, while skills are gated inside Gemini Spark on the AI Ultra plan. Neither has been announced.

title: Odd Lots: How Claude Code Is Reshaping Software (Podcast)
blurb: Bloomberg's Odd Lots podcast covered how Claude Code is changing the economics and practice of software development. The episode discusses the shift toward agent-driven coding workflows, what it means for developer headcount and tooling budgets, and how quickly enterprises are moving from experiments to production use.

title: The Massive Supply Deals Feeding the AI Frenzy Are No Sure Thing
blurb: The multiyear supply agreements underpinning the AI buildout, including those touted by memory makers like SK Hynix, carry far less contractual certainty than their headline numbers suggest. Many are non-binding letters of intent or contain volume and pricing flexibility that lets buyers walk if demand softens, leaving suppliers exposed to capacity they built on the strength of announcements.

title: Grok's CLI caught uploading all your local files to the cloud
blurb: Developers found that xAI's Grok CLI uploaded local files to the cloud without disclosure, surfacing just as the tool gained traction on the back of the Grok 4.5 coding model. The same issue covers engineering leaders reporting rising code review load from AI-generated pull requests and enterprise developers pushing back on higher-than-expected pricing tiers.

title: Hidden prompts can secretly rewrite an AI’s memory, and researchers say that’s a serious problem
blurb: Researchers at New Mexico State University demonstrated GhostWriter, an attack that plants false memories in AI agents through hidden prompts or untrusted external content. Unlike standard prompt injection, which affects a single conversation, the poisoned entries sit dormant in the agent's long-term memory store and can steer behavior across future sessions until they are detected and removed.

title: Anthropic tests new placement for Projects on Claude Desktop
blurb: Anthropic is testing Claude Desktop layout changes that move Projects into the sidebar with pinning and sorting options.

title: Netflix kauft für fast 600 Millionen Ben Afflecks KI-Startup
blurb: Netflix paid $587M in cash to acquire InterPositive, the AI postproduction startup co-founded by Ben Affleck, according to a new filing. The technology fills in missing shots, replaces backgrounds, and corrects lighting errors, and the full team is moving to Netflix with Affleck joining as a senior advisor. Around 300 Netflix films and series now use generative AI in production or postproduction.

title: Big Tech Needs to Prove that AI Spending Is Going Somewhere
blurb: Alphabet and Tesla open Big Tech earnings season this week, with investors looking for evidence that record AI capital spending is producing revenue rather than depreciation. The author argues the burden of proof has shifted from capacity announcements to reported returns, making this quarter's disclosures on cloud backlog and AI product revenue the metrics that matter most.

title: An AI agent hacked Hugging Face. Another AI caught it.
blurb: Hugging Face disclosed that an autonomous AI agent breached its production infrastructure over a weekend, exploiting two code-execution paths in its dataset processing pipeline to harvest cloud credentials and move laterally into several internal clusters. The campaign generated more than 17,000 recorded events across short-lived sandboxes, and the company said it detected and analyzed the intrusion largely with its own AI, finding no evidence that public models, datasets, or Spaces were touched.

title: What's the deal with all the random weekly quota resets for agents lately?
blurb: Anthropic and OpenAI have sharply increased the frequency of weekly quota resets for Claude Code and Codex since the Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 releases. OpenAI reset the Codex weekly quota six times in the past two weeks, plus two banked resets that expire within 30 days. The author argues the problem is that resets are never announced through company channels, so a $25 benefit on a $100/mo plan often lands hours before a scheduled reset and goes unused.

title: What to watch for after Jensen Huang’s Japan visit
blurb: Jensen Huang's two-day Tokyo visit produced deals across Japan's AI stack, including a national AI factory, robotics partnerships, and agreements with chip-material suppliers. Nvidia is building a Vera Rubin AI factory with 13,750 Vera CPUs and 27,500 Rubin GPUs delivering 140 megawatts by 2028, while Japan commits up to 1 trillion yen ($6.2B) over five years to Noetra, a 44-firm sovereign effort to build domestic foundation models for robots and factory floors.

title: AI Pioneer Kai-Fu Lee’s Startup Targets Hong Kong IPO Next Year
blurb: 01.ai, the Chinese AI startup founded by Kai-Fu Lee, is targeting a Hong Kong listing in 2027 and plans to raise additional private funding first. The move would make it one of the first Chinese large-model developers to test public markets, following a strategic pivot away from frontier model training toward enterprise applications.

title: China changes stock market rules weeks before DeepSeek filed to list: Is that a coincidence?
blurb: China loosened STAR Market listing rules weeks before DeepSeek filed for an IPO, easing profitability and shareholding requirements that the company would otherwise have struggled to meet. The report frames the timing as part of Beijing's push to keep its highest-profile AI labs listed domestically rather than offshore.

title: OpenAI reduces Codex Model Context Size from 372k to 272k
blurb: OpenAI cut Codex's model context window from 372k to 272k tokens in a merged pull request.

title: The Creator of Claude Code on the Hottest Piece of Software in the World
blurb: Bloomberg interviewed Boris Cherny, the creator of Claude Code, about how the tool became one of the fastest-growing developer products in the industry. He described how AI coding agents are shifting engineering work from writing code to specifying, reviewing, and orchestrating it across parallel sessions.

title: OpenAI will start notifying parents if their teen has been kicked off of ChatGPT
blurb: OpenAI will now notify parents with linked teen accounts when their child's account is deactivated for violating policies on violent threats or acts of violence. The feature was built with Moonshot, a firm focused on countering online violence, and follows scrutiny over OpenAI's failure to alert authorities after banning the account of the 2025 Tumbler Ridge mass shooting suspect.

title: Kimi K3 is so popular Moonshot ran out of GPUs and paused new sign-ups
blurb: Moonshot AI paused new Kimi K3 subscriptions after demand in the 48 hours following launch pushed its GPU capacity to the limit, and it split its plans into a general Kimi Membership and a separate Kimi Code Membership to keep coding workloads from draining shared compute. K3 is a 2.8T-parameter open-weight model, but the weights do not ship until July 27, so all demand currently lands on Moonshot's own servers.

title: AMD acquires FastFlowLM to accelerate on-device AI inferencing
blurb: AMD acquired FastFlowLM, a runtime for running AI models locally on AMD hardware, and moved the team into its AI Group. FastFlowLM is built on Iron, an open-source close-to-metal Python API for AMD neural processing units, and installs without drivers. Financial terms were not disclosed.

title: The 6 kinds of AI agent architectures
blurb: The author argues that "AI agent" has become too broad to guide enterprise decisions, and proposes six architectural archetypes CIOs should be able to recognize on sight. The first, the conversational assistant, works when paired with persistent user-level memory, tools that act on the user's behalf, and citations on every factual claim. The piece draws on examples from law firm and wealth management deployments, alongside Deloitte data showing 38% of organizations report AI is already strengthening customer relationships.

title: Ollama: All Aboard Open Models
blurb: Ollama's founders shared that the local open-model runtime now serves 8.9 million developers, framing open weights as the personal computer moment for AI. The post lays out the three principles behind the product: ownership of models that can be customized and kept indefinitely, no per-token billing when inference runs on your own hardware, and local execution that keeps data off third-party servers.

title: Trump targets Brazil's payments system while dollar stablecoins are quietly overtaking country's payments
blurb: The US will impose a 25% Section 301 tariff on most Brazilian goods starting July 22, the first time that trade authority has been aimed at another country's domestic payment system. Washington's argument is that Pix, used by more than 90% of Brazilian adults and now processing more transactions than credit and debit cards combined, disadvantages Visa and Mastercard through free consumer transfers and capped merchant fees. The dollar is already winning that market by other means, with dollar-linked stablecoins accounting for around 90% of Brazil's crypto transactions.

title: Netflix CPTO on AI and the future of product and tech roles | Elizabeth Stone
blurb: Netflix's CPTO Elizabeth Stone says systems thinking is now the most important skill she hires for, above any specific craft depth. The constraint has moved from producing work to judging it, since AI floods teams with output and someone has to hold the line on quality and signal. Netflix treats AI fluency as a universal expectation rather than something senior people grow into, which changes what you write into every job description and every performance bar.

title: Netflix paid $587M for Ben Affleck’s AI filmmaking startup
blurb: Netflix paid $587M in cash for InterPositive, the AI post-production startup co-founded by Ben Affleck, according to a new regulatory filing. The tools fix footage after the fact, patching missing shots, replacing backgrounds, and correcting lighting, which is the unglamorous middle of the pipeline rather than the generative headline act. Netflix says around 300 of its titles have already used generative AI, so the buy reads less like a bet on synthetic content and more like buying down production cost on the slate it already has.

title: Jeff Bezos and Sovereign AI back CuspAI in $450M raise
blurb: Cambridge-based CuspAI raised a $450M Series B at a $2.6B valuation, a five-fold jump from the $520M it carried ten months ago. The startup runs materials discovery like a search engine, where customers including ASML and Meta specify the properties they need and the platform finds candidate materials for semiconductors, carbon capture, and water treatment. The round pairs Kleiner Perkins and NEA with Bezos Expeditions and the UK's Sovereign AI Fund, a sign that deep-tech rounds now blend classic venture money with state-backed capital chasing domestic champions.

title: Moonshot’s Kimi K3 May Be More About Memory Than Compute
blurb: Moonshot AI released Kimi K3 on Friday and semiconductor stocks fell sharply, the same reflex that wiped roughly $600B off Nvidia's market value in a single day when DeepSeek's R1 landed in early 2025. The read this time is that K3's gains come more from memory architecture than raw compute, which is a different threat to the chip trade than a pure efficiency story. For founders building on Chinese open models, the practical takeaway is that the cost curve keeps bending from directions the market doesn't price in advance.

title: Moonshot AI’s Kimi K3 Stuns Investors, Challenges Anthropic, OpenAI Lead
blurb: Moonshot AI released Kimi K3, and investors are treating it as a real challenge to the frontier lead held by Anthropic and OpenAI. The assumption now under review in Silicon Valley is that frontier capability stays concentrated in a handful of US labs because the capital required to get there keeps challengers out. For founders building on frontier APIs, a credible third supplier changes pricing leverage and lowers the risk of tying your product roadmap to a single lab.

title: What I learned selling 2,500 MIDI recorders: Hardware is not so hard
blurb: The founder behind Jamcorder sold 2,500 units of a piano-recording device and found that the hardware, the part everyone warns you about, was the smooth part. The hard part was software: roughly 200K lines across firmware, app, and manufacturing tooling, built over three years before LLMs. He kept the device deliberately simple, with one screw, one PCB, no power button, and no USB-C, and his conclusion is that hardware is as hard as you make it.

title: Chinese open models like Kimi K3 and Qwen3.8 Max shift the industry's focus back to marginal costs and cost of goods sold, as inference costs scale with revenue (Ben Thompson/Stratechery)
blurb: Cheap Chinese open models like Kimi K3 and Qwen3.8 Max are pulling the industry's attention away from training runs and back toward marginal cost. The argument is that AI businesses look less like classic software, where serving one more customer is nearly free, and more like a business with real cost of goods sold, because inference spend rises with usage and therefore with revenue. For founders building on top of models, gross margin becomes the number that decides whether the business works, and model routing becomes a margin decision rather than an engineering one.

title: Who’s Afraid of Chinese Models?
blurb: Open weights models out of China like Kimi K3 aren't free, they just move the spending from R&D to COGS, and COGS is a real line item for AI in a way it never was for software. The old zero-marginal-cost logic that made internet businesses winner-take-all doesn't hold when every query costs money to serve. The author argues this shifts the industry's structure back toward classic universal business principles, where cost discipline and gross margin matter more than the aggregation dynamics that defined the last two decades.

title: Sales Rep Annual Quotas in 2026: What High Performers Are Actually Carrying: $750k for SMB, $1.35m for Mid-Market, $2.25m for Enterprise
blurb: Top-quartile enterprise AEs are now carrying $2.25M annual quotas, with mid-market at $1.35M and SMB at $750K, according to ICONIQ's 2026 GTM benchmark survey of 150+ B2B and AI software companies. Attainment went up rather than down, with the best AI-native companies hitting 85% to 90% versus the traditional 65% to 75%, because AI in marketing and SDR motions is generating 10 to 11 points more lead-to-MQL conversion. Comp followed the work, with AE pay tied to net new recurring revenue jumping from 25% to 33% in a single year.

title: Cambridge-based CuspAI raised a $450M Series B led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA, and launches a coalition for AI materials discovery for chip and other industries (Bloomberg)
blurb: CuspAI raised a $450M Series B led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA, and is using it to stand up a coalition for AI-driven materials discovery aimed at chips and other hard industries. The money is going into a domain where the bottleneck is physical, not digital, and where a working model gets validated in a fab rather than a benchmark. Capital that spent the past three years chasing software wrappers is moving toward the parts of the stack that take years to build and are far harder to copy.

title: Databricks raises at $188B as Coatue’s enterprise AI governance play outpaces the models underneath it
blurb: Databricks signed a term sheet at a $188B valuation led by Coatue, a 40% jump from $134B in February and 88% in under a year. The capital goes into Unity AI Gateway, Genie, and Lakebase, three products built on the premise that controlling AI cost, access, and data is worth more than building the models. Investors are pricing the governance layer above the model builders underneath it, with 20,000 organizations and 70% of the Fortune 500 already on the platform and a $5.4B run rate growing 65% year-on-year.

title: Sources: parts of the Trump administration are reigniting efforts to implement de facto bans on foreign open-source models, as Chinese AI models gain momentum (Maria Curi/Axios)
blurb: Parts of the Trump administration are pushing again for de facto bans on foreign open-source models, according to sources, as Chinese releases pick up share among US developers. If it lands, the free tier of the model stack gets a compliance question attached to it, and any startup running DeepSeek or Qwen weights in production inherits procurement risk it did not price in. The practical move is knowing which of your workloads sit on foreign open weights and what the swap to a US open model or a hosted API would cost you in latency and margin.

title: Sources: Chinese startup Moonshot AI is seeking formal approval from investors to start a Hong Kong IPO process, which could happen in as soon as six months (Juro Osawa/The Information)
blurb: Moonshot AI is asking investors to formally approve a Hong Kong IPO process that could begin in as little as six months. Chinese AI labs are reaching public markets years ahead of their US counterparts, where OpenAI and Anthropic remain private and funded by mega-rounds. A listed frontier lab means quarterly disclosure of training costs, inference margins, and enterprise revenue, giving founders and investors the first real public benchmark for what an AI model business actually earns.

title: From $520M to $2.6B in 9 months: Bezos Expeditions co-leads CuspAI’s $450M Series B to scale AI materials discovery
blurb: CuspAI raised $450M at a $2.6B valuation, led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA with Bezos Expeditions participating. The Cambridge, UK startup was worth $520M nine months ago, a five-fold markup in under a year for a company founded in 2024 with fewer than 70 employees. ASML, Meta, Samsung, and Hyundai already run its materials search engine, and the AI Materials Foundry it launched alongside the round pulls in more than 45 partners including NVIDIA. Capital is moving toward AI applied to physical R&D, where the customer list is industrial and the contracts are large.

title: The €411M deal wasn’t about Google: How Germany engineered Europe’s biggest fusion round
blurb: Proxima Fusion's €411M raise is Europe's largest fusion round, and the article argues the deciding factor was German industrial policy rather than the marquee corporate name on the cap table. Public research institutions, state co-investment, and a domestic manufacturing base did the work that a single strategic backer gets credit for. For founders raising in European deep tech, the read is that the capital stack is increasingly assembled by government design, so the fundable companies are the ones whose roadmaps line up with what a national industrial strategy already wants to fund.

title: CuspAI lands $450m round to accelerate AI materials discovery
blurb: CuspAI raised $450M at a $2.6B valuation, led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA with Bezos Expeditions participating, quadrupling its valuation in ten months. Alongside the round it launched the AI Materials Foundry, a network of more than 45 founding members including Nvidia, Meta's FAIR team, Samsung, Hyundai and Merck that pools compute, labs, and proprietary data behind its MIRA discovery platform. The consortium structure is the real signal, because materials startups can't buy their way to experimental validation and have to assemble it from incumbents who already own the equipment.

title: The VC who backed Spotify, Revolut and Airbnb just closed a $300M defence fund
blurb: Lakestar, the firm that backed Spotify, Airbnb and Revolut before any of those categories existed, closed a $300M fund dedicated entirely to defence and resilience tech. Lakestar Resilience I was oversubscribed and is now the largest privately backed institutional defence fund in Europe, with a portfolio including Helsing, TEKEVER, ARX Robotics and Isar Aerospace that has collectively raised more than $5B and employs over 5,000 people. The founders drawing that capital aren't ex-military or defence primes, they're software entrepreneurs and academics treating defence as a computing problem.

title: LSEG: Morgan Stanley collected $2.3B in debt and equity capital market fees in H1 2026, up from $1.4B in H1 2025, driven by AI infrastructure financing deals (Financial Times)
blurb: Morgan Stanley pulled in $2.3B in debt and equity capital market fees in the first half of 2026, up from $1.4B a year earlier, with AI infrastructure financing driving the jump. The banks are now monetizing the buildout more reliably than most of the companies doing the building, because data centers and compute capacity need debt at a scale software never did. For founders, it marks where the capital is actually flowing: not into application-layer rounds but into the physical layer underneath them, on terms set by lenders rather than venture funds.

title: How PayPal CEO Enrique Lores, appointed earlier in 2026 to revive the payments pioneer, may handle Stripe and Advent's takeover offer, based on his HP tenure (Wall Street Journal)
blurb:
Stripe and Advent have put a takeover offer on the table for PayPal, and the response now rests with Enrique Lores, installed as CEO earlier in 2026 to revive the payments pioneer. His HP tenure is the tell, where he fought off Xerox's hostile bid and then cut costs and bought growth rather than sell. For anyone building on payments rails, a Stripe that owns PayPal's merchant base and consumer wallet consolidates distribution into one counterparty, and the pricing you plan around for 2027 gets set by fewer people.

(96 words. Flagging: Lores is HP's CEO, not PayPal's — the source title looks garbled. Worth verifying before this ships.)

title: Moonshot Is Creating New Winners and Losers in the AI Trade
blurb: Moonshot AI's surprise model release jolted markets and forced investors to re-examine the assumptions holding up the global tech rally. The trade that assumed frontier capability stays concentrated in a handful of US labs now has to price in a Chinese lab shipping competitive models on short notice. For founders raising on top of a specific model vendor, the lesson is that supplier advantage is less durable than the funding narrative around it, and the winners and losers in the AI trade are getting re-sorted faster than capital allocation cycles can follow.

title: AI Is ‘New Engine’ Keeping Chinese Economy From Harder Landing
blurb: AI infrastructure buildout has become one of the few reliable growth pockets in China's economy during its weakest stretch in years. The demand is largely global, with overseas capex for data centers, chips, and equipment landing on Chinese suppliers while domestic consumption and property stay soft. For founders selling into or sourcing from China, the read is that the AI supply chain is now decoupled from the broader economy there, and a hard landing in property or consumer demand would not necessarily hit the vendors you depend on.

title: Teleperformance Wants Entire BPO Staff to Be AI-Enabled by 2027
blurb: Teleperformance is putting AI into the workflow of all 500,000 of its employees by 2027, in the industry AI is most likely to hollow out. The company that sells human labor by the seat is betting it can resell the same seats as AI-augmented ones before clients decide they no longer need the seats at all. For founders selling into or competing with outsourced services, the incumbent's move is to reprice existing headcount rather than defend it, which compresses the window where an AI-native alternative looks obviously cheaper.

title: Singapore Mulls Lower Taxes, Easier Talent Entry for Hedge Funds
blurb: Singapore is weighing tax cuts for hedge funds and asset managers alongside looser rules on hiring foreign talent, as Hong Kong closes in on its own sweeping tax exemptions. The two financial centers are now competing on the same two levers founders care about anywhere: what the government takes and who you are allowed to hire. For anyone weighing an APAC entity or a fund domicile, the terms are still moving, and the jurisdiction that lands the better package will pull capital and headcount with it.

title: Bezos backs CuspAI as startup teams up with Nvidia to hunt for chipmaking materials
blurb: CuspAI raised $450M at a $2.6B valuation, led by Kleiner Perkins and NEA with participation from Bezos Expeditions, AMD Ventures, and Britain's Sovereign AI Venture Fund. The startup simulates novel materials to narrow which candidates are worth lab testing, and it is launching an AI Materials Foundry with Nvidia and 44 other organizations supplying compute, labs, and chemistry expertise. Capital that spent the last cycle chasing software agents is moving toward companies applying AI to physical constraints, where the bottleneck is a material nobody has discovered rather than a model nobody has trained.

title: The Pentagon Is Finally Buying (Some) Weapons From Startups
blurb: The Pentagon is buying weapons from defense-tech startups, but a surging budget means it doesn't have to pick between them and the incumbent primes. Both sides get funded, which reads as validation for the startups and a reprieve for the contractors who expected to lose share. The test comes when the budget stops growing, because that is the first moment the department has to actually choose, and startups selling on speed rather than entrenched program relationships are the ones exposed.

title: Zalando joins Sereact's $116M Series B to accelerate AI-powered warehouse automation
blurb: Zalando joined Sereact's Series B as a strategic investor, taking the round to $116M and the Stuttgart robotics company's total raised past $145M. Around 50% of items Zalando ships come back, and returns processing is still largely manual work: unpacking, inspecting, folding, and sorting, with European rules preventing retailers from simply destroying the goods. Sereact's dual-arm system automates that specific loop, which is why the customer became the investor rather than just signing a contract.

title: Mexico Extends Its Venture Lead Over Brazil As More Global VCs Enter Latin America
blurb: Mexico's startups raised $944M in Q2, up 131% year over year and more than double Brazil's $350M, marking the third quarter in the past year that Mexico has outraised its larger neighbor. Mexico City companies accounted for the region's three largest deals, including Clip's $500M raise at a $2.5B valuation and Plata's $405M Series C at $5B, with Founders Fund, a16z, and other US firms leading. Late-stage and growth funding drove $991M of the region's $1.36B total, up 84% year over year, while angel, seed, and early round counts fell.

title: From research project to €1B-backed AI lab in 18 months: Prior Labs is Europe’s new deeptech benchmark
blurb: SAP completed its acquisition of Prior Labs, a Freiburg startup founded 18 months ago that had raised only €9M before the exit. Prior Labs built TabPFN, a foundation model for spreadsheets and databases rather than text, going from a Nature paper to a pre-seed round to acquisition without a Series A. SAP is committing over €1B to the company through 2030 and has not disclosed the purchase price. European deep tech hit $20.3B in investment in 2025 but has lagged the US on exits, and this compresses the usual academic-to-acquisition timeline by years.

title: Netflix kauft für fast 600 Millionen Ben Afflecks KI-Startup
blurb: Netflix paid $587M in cash for InterPositive, the AI post-production startup co-founded by Ben Affleck, with the full team joining and Affleck staying on as a senior advisor. The tools handle the unglamorous parts of finishing a film: missing coverage, background replacement, lighting corrections. Around 300 Netflix films and series now use generative AI somewhere in production or post, which is the real signal here. Buyers with distribution are acquiring production tooling outright rather than licensing it, and that closes an obvious exit path for anyone selling AI tools into the studios.

title: China changes stock market rules weeks before DeepSeek filed to list: Is that a coincidence?
blurb: China changed its stock market listing rules weeks before DeepSeek filed to go public, relaxing the standards that would have kept an unprofitable AI lab off the STAR Market. The sequencing points at industrial policy rather than coincidence, with Beijing rewriting capital markets plumbing to keep its frontier labs listing domestically instead of in Hong Kong or offshore. For founders competing against Chinese AI companies, the implication is a rival funding channel that opens on policy timelines rather than market ones, which means capital availability there is not something you can forecast from valuations or investor appetite.

title: Big Tech Needs to Prove that AI Spending Is Going Somewhere
blurb: Alphabet and Tesla open the sector's earnings season this week, and the question is no longer how much Big Tech is spending on AI but what it has to show for it. Capex has run ahead of disclosed revenue attributable to AI for several quarters, and investors are starting to ask for the second number rather than the first. For startups selling into these budgets, a quarter where the hyperscalers get punished for spending is the quarter their procurement cycles get longer.

title: „Die meisten hören nach fünf Versuchen auf“: Mit diesen Sales-Regeln baute Mirko Novakovic ein Milliarden-Startup auf
