blurb: Most founders give up after five attempts to reach a prospect, which is where Mirko Novakovic says the deals actually start. The German serial founder built Codecentric, sold Instana to IBM for around $500M in 2020, and raised $110M for Dash0 in March 2026 to reach unicorn status, and he credits the phone as his most important sales tool. His rule is that persistence past the point most people quit is the cheapest competitive advantage in enterprise sales.

title: How Daniel Ek is building his ‘new European dream’
blurb: Daniel Ek is putting his Spotify wealth behind a European industrial bet, backing companies like Neko Health and Helsing through his investment vehicle rather than chasing the US model. Both have raised at higher valuations than most of their American competitors, which inverts the usual assumption that European founders take a discount. For operators building in Europe, the read is that capital and valuation ceilings are no longer the binding constraint in defense and health.

title: Claude Code uses Bun written in Rust now
blurb: Claude Code v2.1.181 and later ship the Rust port of Bun, which Jarred Sumner says made startup 10% faster on Linux with no other user-visible change. The author confirmed it locally by running strings on the Claude binary, which reports Bun v1.4.0 and lists 563 Rust source paths including src/bundler/bundle_v2.rs, a version that only exists in Bun canary.

title: Transcribe.cpp
blurb: Transcribe.cpp is a ggml-based speech-to-text library covering 16 ASR families and 60+ models, with acceleration via Vulkan, Metal, CUDA, and TinyBLAS. The author, who maintains the Handy desktop app, built it as a near drop-in whisper.cpp replacement after hitting the limits of ONNX's CPU-only performance, and every model ships numerically validated and WER tested against its reference implementation.

title: Setting up your spare Mac for Claude Code to control, a step-by-step guide
blurb: The guide walks through configuring a spare Mac as an always-on host for Claude Code with computer use enabled, reachable from the Claude mobile app or over SSH from a main machine. The setup trades container isolation for full macOS access, letting the agent drive Mac-only apps like Unity and run with `--dangerously-skip-permissions` on hardware that has nothing worth losing.

title: Regressive JPEGs
blurb: The author abused JPEG's progressive mode, where a file is split into scans that each fill in a spectral range, to concatenate multiple same-resolution images into one file that animates as it downloads over a slow connection. The trick caps out fast because most decoders stop after roughly nine scans as a zip-bomb guard, and baseline single-scan frames fail since progressive scans cannot mix DC and AC coefficients.

title: LoRA Speedrun – a public wall-clock leaderboard for fine-tuning techniques
blurb: LoRA Speedrun is a public wall-clock leaderboard for LoRA fine-tuning, with two frozen tracks: Qwen2.5-1.5B to 57% on GSM8K, and a SQuAD task, both on a single L40S. Records are re-run 3x with fresh seeds on identical hardware before they count, and timing runs on a Modal sandbox covered by free monthly credits, so anyone can verify any record with one command.

title: Hugging Face Breached Through a Malicious Dataset: What the Patches Reveal
blurb: Hugging Face disclosed a compromise of part of its production infrastructure that started with a malicious dataset abusing a remote-code loader and jinja2 template injection in a dataset config, then escalated to node-level access and lateral movement across internal clusters. Public dataset-viewer commits show the response: an fsspec protocol allowlist, worker pods stripped of ServiceAccount tokens and Linux capabilities, and static S3 and Mongo credentials replaced with IRSA auth.

title: SleeperGem Uses Three Malicious RubyGems Packages to Target Developer Machines
blurb: StepSecurity flagged three malicious RubyGems packages, dubbed SleeperGem, that impersonate Microsoft's Git Credential Manager or revive gems dormant since 2019. The loader checks roughly 30 CI environment variables from GitHub Actions, GitLab, CircleCI, and Jenkins, exiting if any are found, so it only drops its native daemon and persistence on developer machines rather than ephemeral runners.

title: Three checks that lied to me while building a content extraction API
blurb: The author traces three green checks on a content extraction API that asserted the opposite of reality: a 200-character success threshold that scored navigation menus as articles, a sandbox verification script that inherited Playwright's own `--no-sandbox` default, and a "clean" virtualenv that reused cached Chromium binaries. Re-scoring against a 100-word floor dropped extraction success from 90% to 72%.

title: UnifiedIR for Julia
blurb: A WIP PR proposes UnifiedIR, a new top-level Julia package that replaces SyntaxTree, SyntaxGraph, CodeInfo, and IRCode with a single dialect-aware, region-based IR in the style of MLIR. The core structure is extensible with per-statement columns and layered views, including an IncrementalCompact-style one-pass rewrite view and an LLVM-like linked-list view that uses an order maintenance algorithm to avoid quadratic behavior on large functions.

title: Beyond grep: The case for a context-rich AI coding harness
blurb: Claude Code's product team argues for a "lean harness," deliberately shipping fewer opinionated tools rather than pre-building structured context indexes over a codebase. Head of product Cat Wu says evals show no measurable gain from advance codebase indexing, and that models improve fast enough that opinionated tooling built around today's limitations becomes dead weight, leaving developers to add their own context tools when they want them.

title: My trading system has a 73.5% win rate and loses money. Here is the diagnostic that found it.
blurb: The author audited a live FX system over 16 years and 7,641 trades, tracing a 73.5% win rate that still lost 4,669 pips to two bugs: costs subtracted post-hoc rather than charged at the fill, and a 0.25-0.4 reward:risk ratio that puts breakeven at 75-82%. The reusable diagnostic is a Spearman rank correlation between train and test parameter grids per walk-forward fold, separating a bad optimizer from an unlearnable surface.

title: The Database Is Not the Data Model
blurb: The author argues that schema design and data modeling are different disciplines, and that the industry collapsed them because DDL was visible, executable, and committable while conceptual models were not. The piece traces the drift from Codd's data independence and Chen's ER modeling through ORMs shipping `models.py` and dbt calling a SELECT statement a model. The result is that "model" now means an ERD, a table definition, a SQL transform, and a transformer, which leaves teams arguing about implementation before they have agreed on what the entities mean.

title: Cities aren't malls. So why are they starting to look like them?
blurb: The authors argue that civic wayfinding has fallen into "mall-ification," where signage systems in Ohio, Texas, and Los Angeles read as interchangeable because they optimize for function alone. They point to Nicolas Vrignaud's work at Docks en Seine and Bruce Mau's Toronto Carpet Factory identity as counterexamples, where material, scale, and form are drawn from the site's own history rather than applied on top of it.

title: KPF wraps China skyscraper in one-kilometer-long "landscape ribbon"
blurb: KPF designed TP-Link LXD in Shenzhen, a 124,600-square-metre campus of three interconnected towers wrapped by a one-kilometre ribbon of terraces and planted staircases. The continuous circulation route turns the compact site's limited ground plane into stacked shared green space, threading offices, R&D floors, and housing together rather than stacking them as separate volumes.

title: YN Studio extends London home around light-filled stairwell
blurb: YN Studio clad a Hackney terrace extension in recycled plastic tiles that imitate slate, organized around a top-lit stairwell.

title: Ortega Diago creates Brutalist changing rooms for San Sebastian factory
blurb: Ortega Diago turned a narrow warehouse strip in San Sebastian into board-formed concrete changing rooms for 100-plus factory workers.

title: Vector Architects rejects "white cube" design for Aranya Art Center Guangzhou
blurb: Vector Architects broke the Aranya Art Center Guangzhou into free-standing galleries arranged around a preserved 50-meter avenue of 25 banyan trees, rejecting the enclosed white-cube museum plan. The main hall pairs textured glass brick outside with translucent polycarbonate inside to pull in daylight while holding a climate boundary against south China's heat and humidity.

title: WordPress Core "wp2shell" RCE flaws get public exploits, patch now
blurb: Public proof-of-concept exploits are out for wp2shell, a chain of CVE-2026-63030 (REST API batch-route confusion) and CVE-2026-60137 (SQL injection in WP_Query's author__not_in) that gives pre-auth RCE on stock WordPress 6.9.0-6.9.4 and 7.0.0-7.0.1. WordPress forced auto-updates to 7.0.2 and 6.9.5; sites with auto-update disabled should patch and hunt for webshells now.

title: Two new high severity WordPress vulnerabilities, patch immediately!
blurb: WordPress patched two SQL injection flaws, CVE-2026-60137 and CVE-2026-63030, the latter a REST API batch-route confusion issue that chains to remote code execution on 6.9 and the 7.1 beta. Fixes ship in 6.9.5, 6.8.6, and 7.1 beta2; sites that cannot update immediately should block anonymous access to /wp-json/batch/v1 and ?rest_route=/batch/v1 at the WAF as a temporary measure.

title: Exploit brokers pay $500k for WordPress RCEs. I found one with GPT5.6 and $25
blurb: A Searchlight Cyber researcher used GPT5.6 Sol Ultra, running four agents for six hours at roughly $25 in inference, to find a pre-auth RCE chain in WordPress core from source analysis alone. Two other teams independently reproduced the full chain during the disclosure hold and PoCs have since appeared on GitHub, so patch immediately and treat unpatched internet-facing instances as exposed.

title: World's Largest AI Model Repository Hugging Face Breached by Autonomous AI Agent
blurb: Hugging Face disclosed that an autonomous agent framework breached its production infrastructure, chaining remote code execution in its dataset loader and a template injection in a dataset config to reach node-level access, then harvesting cloud and cluster credentials and moving laterally over a weekend. Public models, datasets, and Spaces appear untouched, but internal datasets and service credentials were exposed, so rotate account access tokens and audit recent activity.

title: Hugging Face Breached Through a Malicious Dataset: What the Patches Reveal
blurb: Hugging Face disclosed a July 16 compromise of part of its production infrastructure that began with a malicious dataset abusing a remote-code loader and jinja2 template injection in a dataset config, giving the attacker worker execution, node-level access, and cloud and cluster credentials. No CVE or payload was published, but dataset-viewer commits show an fsspec protocol allowlist, disabled ServiceAccount token mounting, seccomp RuntimeDefault, and a move to IRSA for S3 and MongoDB auth.

title: Critical ServiceNow code execution flaw now exploited in attacks
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-6875, a pre-auth sandbox-escape RCE in the ServiceNow AI Platform, with Defused observing in-the-wild attempts against the /assessment_thanks.do sink starting July 17. ServiceNow patched hosted instances and shipped self-hosted updates on July 13 but still lists the flaw as not known-exploited, so self-hosted operators should patch now rather than wait for the advisory to change.

title: Critical NGINX Vulnerability Can Crash Workers and May Allow Remote Code Execution
blurb: F5 patched CVE-2026-42533 (CVSS v4 9.2), a heap buffer overflow in nginx's script engine that a remote, unauthenticated attacker can trigger with crafted HTTP requests to crash workers or, where ASLR is bypassable, achieve RCE. Every build from 0.9.6 through 1.31.2 is affected, but exploitation requires a regex-based map whose output is referenced after an earlier capture, so audit configs before assuming you are clear.

title: Capital One Open Sources AI-Powered ‘VulnHunter’ Security Tool
blurb: Capital One open sourced VulnHunter, an agentic code-analysis tool that traces exploitable paths and proposes targeted patches rather than emitting passive scanner findings. It requires Claude Opus 4.8 and a working Claude Code environment, so evaluation costs scale with repo count; Capital One says it ran across thousands of internal repositories, and GitHub ships with quickstart docs and example remediation workflows.

title: Russian-Speaking Hacker Uses Google Gemini CLI to Control Botnet of Eight Dental Clinic PCs
blurb: Trend Micro analyzed 200 Gemini CLI session logs from March 19 to April 21 showing solo threat actor "bandcampro" using Google's agent to migrate C&C infrastructure, crack passwords, and operate a botnet of eight dental clinic PCs with access to their OpenDental database. The actor bypassed Gemini's guardrails by claiming to be an authorized pentester, and the entire C&C stack fit in three plaintext files totaling roughly 5 KB.

title: Meet Dusseldorf, Microsoft’s open-source out-of-band security platform
blurb: Microsoft released Dusseldorf, an open-source out-of-band application security testing platform that runs DNS, HTTP, and HTTPS listeners on an operator-controlled domain to catch SSRF, blind XSS, SSTI, and XXE callbacks. Deployment is self-hosted via Docker Compose for lab use or Helm on Azure, which keeps interaction data off third-party collaborator services that most teams currently rely on for the same detection.

title: On Flock License Plate Tracking Cameras
blurb: A Flock ALPR partial-plate match led police to stop and arrest a driver whose New Jersey manufacturer plate, 34 10 DTM, matched a hotlist entry recorded only as 34 DTM. Flock says the behavior is intended, since law enforcement requests substring matching on partial plates, meaning any vehicle sharing a fleet plate structure gets flagged nationwide until the originating agency corrects its report.

title: New 7-Zip Vulnerability Could Let Crafted XZ Archives Run Code During Extraction
blurb: A heap-based buffer overflow in 7-Zip's XZ decoder (CVE-2026-14266, CVSS 7.0) lets a crafted archive execute code in the extracting process, which ZDI detailed on July 15 after a fix shipped June 25 in version 26.02. The vector is local and user-interaction-required, not no-click, and the flawed length handling in MixCoder_Code dates back to at least 21.07, so update to 26.02 to also pick up April's NTFS-handler fixes.

title: Ernst & Young Data Breach Affects Personal, Financial Information
blurb: Ernst & Young began notifying clients of a breach at a third-party service management platform used for tax work, where attackers had access between March 28 and April 12 and downloaded client documents containing names, addresses, Social Security numbers, and account and card numbers. EY has not disclosed the intrusion vector or a threat actor, and no ransomware or extortion group has claimed the incident.

title: CISA urges immediate SharePoint hardening as exploits mount
blurb: CISA added three actively exploited Microsoft SharePoint Server flaws to the KEV catalog, including CVE-2026-56164, an elevation-of-privilege bug that is remotely exploitable without authentication despite its 5.3 CVSS score. Microsoft has shipped updates for supported versions and recommends enabling AMSI integration, but CISA notes patching alone may not evict attacker persistence, so defenders should hunt for IOCs and rotate SharePoint machine keys.

title: Microsoft warns of surge in ACR Stealer attacks on customers
blurb: Microsoft observed a surge in ACR Stealer infections against enterprise customers between late April and mid-June, with ClickFix lures delivering the payload via WebDAV shares and MSHTA. The malware-as-a-service operation, a suspected Amatera Stealer rebrand, decrypts browser credentials through DPAPI and exfiltrates session tokens, so defenders should block rundll32 and mshta execution from WebDAV paths and rotate exposed browser-stored credentials.

title: Update now: 7-Zip fixes RCE flaw exploitable with malicious archives
blurb: 7-Zip 26.02 patches a heap-based buffer overflow in XZ decompression that allows arbitrary code execution when a user opens a crafted archive. There are no reports of active exploitation, but 7-Zip has no auto-update mechanism, so defenders should push the build from 7-zip.org manually and watch for phishing campaigns delivering malicious archives.

title: SleeperGem Uses Three Malicious RubyGems Packages to Target Developer Machines
blurb: StepSecurity flagged SleeperGem, a RubyGems supply-chain campaign in which three malicious gems, including a typosquat of Microsoft's Git Credential Manager, fetch a second-stage payload from an attacker-controlled Forgejo host and install a persistent native daemon. The loader checks roughly 30 CI environment variables and exits on GitHub Actions, GitLab, Jenkins, and similar runners, so build-log telemetry will miss it. Audit developer workstations for the affected versions and rotate any Git credentials handled by those machines.

title: A forensic tool for backdoored code completions in AI assistants
blurb: Researchers at the University of Louisville and the University of North Texas released CodeTracer, a forensic tool that traces a poisoned code completion back to the fine-tuning examples that taught it, without access to training gradients. It summarizes the unsafe behavior, uses UniXcoder to narrow a corpus of over a million Python files to 500 candidates, then has GPT-4.1 judge which ones carry the same pattern. That covers the post-incident gap left by data screening and output scanning, which only catch poisoning before it lands.

title: WP2Shell WordPress Vulnerabilities Exploited in the Wild
blurb: Two WordPress flaws dubbed WP2Shell, CVE-2026-60137 (SQL injection) and CVE-2026-63030 (arbitrary code execution), are being chained for unauthenticated RCE against stock installs running 6.9.0-6.9.4 and 7.0.0-7.0.1. WordPress shipped 6.9.5 and 7.0.2 and forced auto-updates, but public PoCs and confirmed exploitation by Patchstack, Hexastrike, and WatchTowr mean unpatched sites should be treated as compromised.

title: Kenya investigates hack of Ruto’s official website after bitcoin ransom demand
blurb: Attackers defaced president.go.ke over the weekend, replacing the Kenyan president's homepage with a ransom note demanding five bitcoin (roughly $317,000) and threatening to leak material. Kenya's NC4 pulled the site offline Saturday, restored it Sunday, and says it found no evidence of data exfiltration; no group has claimed responsibility and officials have not disclosed the initial access vector.

title: Hugging Face experienced cyberattack carried out end-to-end by agentic AI
blurb: Hugging Face disclosed a breach it attributes to an attack chain executed end-to-end by agentic AI, and has since secured the affected systems and contacted law enforcement. Registered users are being directed to rotate credentials and access tokens, and teams pulling models or datasets from the Hub should audit recent token usage and CI pipelines that authenticate against it.

title: Shark vacuums with flawed Amazon policy can easily expose millions of user data
blurb: A researcher found that Shark's Amazon Alexa skill integration uses an overly permissive account-linking policy, letting an attacker who knows a target's email enumerate and access device and account data across millions of vacuum owners. No patch has shipped, so operators with Shark devices on managed networks should unlink the Alexa skill and treat associated account credentials as exposed.

title: Qubes OS Security in the Public Record
blurb: A longitudinal analysis of 109 Qubes Security Bulletins (2011-2025) and the project's Xen Security Advisory tracker found that 87 of 109 QSBs (79.8%) trace to Xen, CPU/microarchitectural, or other upstream components rather than Qubes-core logic, with 113 of 464 XSAs affecting Qubes. Post-2018 annual disclosure rates are statistically flat, and S-shaped vulnerability discovery models did not beat a rolling-mean baseline on short-horizon forecasts. The measurable risk in compartmentalized architectures sits in the upstream trust anchors, not the isolation layer.

title: Grok's CLI caught uploading all your local files to the cloud
blurb: The Grok CLI was caught uploading local files from developer machines to xAI's cloud, surfacing just as the capable Grok 4.5 coding model was driving adoption of the tool. Teams that installed it should treat any secrets, .env files, and source in reachable directories as exposed, rotate credentials, and block the binary until xAI documents what was collected and how long it is retained.

title: This Android lock screen bug lets anyone text using Gemini without knowing your PIN
blurb: A lock screen authentication bypass on Android 16 devices lets anyone with physical access send SMS and WhatsApp messages through Gemini without entering a PIN, by pressing Continue and Add Attachment simultaneously. Typing @WhatsApp into Gemini's field also silently reconnects previously revoked app integrations. Google confirmed a fix is rolling out this week; until it lands, disable Gemini lock screen access on managed fleets.

title: Hidden prompts can secretly rewrite an AI’s memory, and researchers say that’s a serious problem
blurb: Researchers at New Mexico State University demonstrated GhostWriter, an attack that plants false entries in an AI agent's long-term memory store via hidden prompts or untrusted external content. Unlike single-session prompt injection, the poisoned memory persists across sessions and activates on later legitimate requests, so defenders running agents with persistent memory should treat memory writes as untrusted input and audit stored entries.

title: Apple @ Work: New macOS ClickFix malware brings a new potential backdoor to your enterprise fleet
blurb: Netskope Threat Labs documented a macOS ClickFix campaign that uses fake optimization utilities, spoofed GitHub repos, and localized IT support pages to trick users into pasting a Terminal command that loads an AppleScript stealer entirely in memory. The payload then spoofs a System Preferences password prompt to unlock the keychain and exfiltrate saved credentials and session cookies, so defenders should alert on clipboard-sourced Terminal execution and treat affected keychains as compromised.

title: New Index Tracks Material Breaches — And Refuses to Add Up the Losses
blurb: Richard Bird launched the Hacker in a Hoodie Index, a pair of ledgers tracking material breach disclosures from SEC 8-K filings and public reporting, currently covering more than 100 incidents. Each entry is graded by sourcing strength (verified, attested, or inferred) and the site deliberately withholds an aggregate loss figure, since most disclosures never name a dollar amount.

title: Chrome 150 Update Patches Severe Memory Safety Bugs
blurb: Google released Chrome 150 (150.0.7871.128/.129 on Windows and macOS, 150.0.7871.128 on Linux) to patch seven memory safety bugs, including three critical use-after-free flaws in CameraCapture, GPU, and Network, plus an out-of-bounds read/write in V8 reported by OpenAI Codex Security. None are known to be exploited, but Chrome UAF bugs are a routine exploitation target, so push the update through managed fleets now.

title: An AI agent hacked Hugging Face. Another AI caught it.
blurb: Hugging Face disclosed on July 16 that an autonomous AI agent chained two code-execution paths in its dataset ingestion pipeline to run code on a processing worker, then harvested cloud and cluster credentials and moved laterally across several internal clusters over one weekend, generating more than 17,000 logged events from rotating short-lived sandboxes. Public models, datasets, Spaces, and container images were unaffected, but users should rotate access tokens and audit recent account activity.

title: The Windows 10 hangover is becoming a security problem
blurb: Windows 10 still runs on 16.9% of Windows devices nine months after end of support, and those machines average 1,903 active CVEs each versus 652 on Windows 11, with 66.6% rated high or critical and 2.4% known-exploited. Healthcare, retail, and manufacturing lag most because embedded and vendor-certified hardware can't take Windows 11. Consumer ESU buys eligible devices until October 2027 but excludes most enterprise, LTSC, and domain-joined configurations, so segment and compensate rather than assume coverage.

title: Craneware plc hit by cyber security breach
blurb: Craneware, an AIM-listed healthcare financial software provider, disclosed unauthorized access to a subset of its data environment, with a significant volume of file names plus some employee, customer, and partner records exfiltrated. The company says the incident is contained with no residual indicators of compromise, and has notified the ICO and FBI; customers should expect follow-on phishing using the exfiltrated file names and partner data.

title: WordPress Core "wp2shell" RCE flaws get public exploits, patch now
blurb: Public proof-of-concept exploits are now available for "wp2shell," a chain of two WordPress Core flaws (CVE-2026-63030 and CVE-2026-60137) that gives unauthenticated attackers remote code execution on stock installs running 6.9.0-6.9.4 and 7.0.0-7.0.1. WordPress has forced auto-updates to 7.0.2 and 6.9.5, but IT teams running self-hosted or version-pinned instances need to patch manually across an estimated 500M sites.

title: Two new high severity WordPress vulnerabilities, patch immediately!
blurb: WordPress patched two vulnerabilities in 6.9.5 and 6.8.6, including CVE-2026-63030, a REST API batch-route confusion and SQL injection flaw that allows remote code execution on 6.9 installs. Teams that cannot patch immediately can block anonymous access to /wp-json/batch/v1 at the WAF level, though Searchlight Cyber warns this may break legitimate traffic and should stay temporary.

title: Microsoft warns of surge in ACR Stealer attacks on customers
blurb: Microsoft reported a surge in ACR Stealer infections across its enterprise customers between late April and mid-June, with attackers using ClickFix lures, WebDAV shares, and MSHTA to deliver a malware-as-a-service payload that harvests browser passwords, session cookies, and Microsoft 365 documents. Stolen authentication tokens bypass MFA outright, so affected tenants need session revocation and credential rotation, not just endpoint cleanup.

title: Microsoft confirms WSUS service degradation impacting all versions of Windows
blurb: Microsoft confirmed a service degradation in Windows Server Update Services affecting all supported Windows versions, disrupting the patch-distribution path many enterprises still rely on for managed update rollouts. Admins running on-premises WSUS rather than Intune or Autopatch face delayed security updates until Microsoft ships a fix, which strengthens the case for the cloud update management Microsoft has been steering customers toward.

title: Meet Dusseldorf, Microsoft’s open-source out-of-band security platform
blurb: Microsoft released Dusseldorf, an open-source out-of-band application security testing platform that runs DNS, HTTP, and HTTPS listeners on an operator-controlled domain to catch SSRF, XSS, template injection, and XXE activity at the network edge. Security teams that have been assembling this infrastructure in-house get a self-hosted alternative to commercial OAST tooling, with local Docker Compose and Azure Helm deployment paths.

title: Critical NGINX Vulnerability Can Crash Workers and May Allow Remote Code Execution
blurb: F5 patched CVE-2026-42533, a heap buffer overflow in nginx's script engine that lets an unauthenticated attacker crash worker processes with crafted HTTP requests and, where ASLR can be bypassed, potentially execute code. Fixes landed in nginx 1.30.4, 1.31.3, and NGINX Plus 37.0.3.1, but F5 has not yet published fixed builds for Ingress Controller, Gateway Fabric, App Protect WAF, or Instance Manager.

title: Critical ServiceNow code execution flaw now exploited in attacks
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-6875, a pre-auth sandbox-escape RCE in the ServiceNow AI Platform, days after ServiceNow patched hosted instances and shipped fixes for self-hosted deployments on July 13th. ServiceNow's advisory still says it is unaware of active exploitation, so self-hosted customers running the platform that serves 85% of the Fortune 500 should patch on their own timeline rather than the vendor's.

title: Major glitch in Matrix as UK government projects watchdog rates shared services cluster red
blurb: The UK government's projects watchdog gave a red rating to Matrix, the shared services cluster consolidating back-office ERP and HR systems across multiple Whitehall departments, meaning delivery appears unachievable without a reset of scope, schedule, or budget. Multi-department ERP consolidation is the same pattern vendors sell to large enterprises, and the red flag lands on the delivery model rather than the software itself.

title: Update now: 7-Zip fixes RCE flaw exploitable with malicious archives
blurb: 7-Zip released version 26.02 to patch a heap-based buffer overflow in its XZ decompression path that lets a malicious archive execute arbitrary code as the opening user. 7-Zip ships with no auto-update mechanism, so IT teams need to push 26.02 manually across endpoints, and prior 7-Zip and WinRAR archive flaws were weaponized in phishing campaigns within months of disclosure.

title: AI ops tools will create console sprawl and break IT more often: Gartner
blurb: Gartner warned that the wave of vendor-specific AI ops tools will add consoles rather than consolidate them, leaving IT teams with more surfaces to watch and more frequent outages as agents act across systems. Buyers evaluating AIOps add-ons from incumbent monitoring and ITSM vendors should weigh integration and consolidation terms now, before each platform ships its own agent dashboard into the stack.

title: Microsoft confirms Windows Server Update Services sync delays
blurb: Microsoft confirmed a WSUS synchronization bug affecting Windows 10 1607 and later and Windows Server 2012 and later, with sync timeouts starting July 13 and blocking update deployment through WSUS and Configuration Manager. Saturday's mitigation restored new and rebuilt WSUS installations, but previously affected servers still need manual removal of the bad metadata, and Microsoft has not shipped those steps yet.

title: Auditors tell UK government to do the math before banking on £45B AI savings
blurb: The UK's National Audit Office told the government it lacks the baseline data and measurement framework to verify the £45B in annual savings ministers have projected from AI adoption across public services. The finding lands as departments negotiate AI contracts with Microsoft, Google, and other vendors, and it raises the odds that procurement teams get asked to justify spend against benchmarks nobody has actually established.

title: Windows KB5121767 OOB update fixes shutdowns on some Dell PCs
blurb: Microsoft released out-of-band updates KB5121767 and KB5121768 to fix unexpected shutdowns, thermal throttling, and battery drain on Dell PCs running Windows 11 25H2, 24H2, and Enterprise LTSC 2024, caused by a conflict between the new Windows USB-C Connection Manager and Intel's IPF Processor Participant driver. Autopatch fleets with hotpatching get the fix automatically, while everyone else needs to pull it manually and only on affected devices.

title: NetApp expands storage-layer AI processing with DataPelago deal
blurb: NetApp acquired DataPelago, whose Nucleus engine processes data across CPUs and GPUs at the storage layer instead of moving it to external compute clusters, for undisclosed terms. NetApp claims the technology cuts infrastructure costs by up to 80% and removes the copy step between operational systems and AI pipelines, positioning ONTAP customers against Nvidia's GPU-only cuDF framework.

title: Airbus takes flight from AWS. What happens next is critical
blurb: Airbus is moving off AWS, and the author argues that what follows the decision matters more than the decision itself. Large enterprise cloud exits tend to stall midway, leaving the company paying for the old hyperscaler contract and the new environment at once. For IT leaders weighing a sovereignty- or cost-driven migration, the exit plan deserves the same scrutiny as the original vendor selection.

title: High hardware costs see Red Hat offer a two-server edge rig, no mini-PC required
blurb: Red Hat introduced a two-server edge configuration for OpenShift, dropping the third control-plane node that its compact cluster deployments previously required as hardware costs climb. IT teams running hundreds of distributed sites can cut per-location server spend without falling back to single-node clusters, which trade away the failover that retail, manufacturing, and telco edge workloads depend on.

title: The Windows 10 hangover is becoming a security problem
blurb: Windows 10 still runs on 16.9% of Windows devices nine months after Microsoft ended support, and those machines average 1,903 active CVEs each compared with 652 on Windows 11, according to Lansweeper. Consumer Extended Security Updates buy time until October 2027 but exclude most enterprise, LTSC, and domain-joined configurations, leaving healthcare, retail, and manufacturing fleets tied to certified hardware without a patching path.

title: UK tech trade associations hit out amidst reports DSIT could be scrapped
blurb: UK tech trade bodies including techUK and the Startup Coalition publicly opposed reported plans by incoming prime minister Andy Burnham to abolish the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology and fold tech policy into a larger business department. The reorg would put the AI Safety Institute, Sovereign AI Fund, GDS, and UKRI under new ownership, creating months of uncertainty for vendors and public-sector buyers with active UK digital transformation contracts.

title: The 6 kinds of AI agent architectures
blurb: The author argues that "AI agent" has become meaningless as a procurement term and lays out six architectural archetypes CIOs should be able to identify on sight, starting with the conversational assistant. The practical value is in scoping: a chat-based assistant with persistent memory and citations solves a different problem than an autonomous planning system, and buying the wrong one wastes budget. Deloitte found 38% of organizations report AI is already improving customer relationships.

title: The audit trail CIOs need before the next cyber crisis
blurb: The author argues that DORA, NIS2, and the SEC's cyber disclosure rules have shifted CIO liability from operating controls to proving, after the fact, that leadership decisions matched the risk evidence available at the time. Green dashboards that mask legacy debt, undocumented service accounts, and shadow cloud instances become evidence against you once regulators and defense counsel start reconstructing the timeline. The recommended output is a documented audit trail of how risks were escalated and decided.

title: Cisco buying bargain Israeli startup for $150M+: report
blurb: Cisco is reportedly in talks to acquire Israeli exposure management vendor Zafran Security for $150M+, below the $200M valuation Zafran carried in its December round after ARR stalled near $20M. Zafran denies the deal and calls it a strategic investment, but the platform would slot into Splunk's risk analytics and backfill the gap Cisco left when it shuttered Kenna.

title: Shipyard: How We Built Slack's Next-Generation EC2 Platform
blurb: Slack's infrastructure team detailed Shipyard, the EC2 platform that replaced continuous Chef convergence on long-lived instances with immutable, deployable artifacts and progressive rollouts. The team hit the limits of configuration management across tens of thousands of instances, where drift was inevitable and service-level deploys required coordinating changes across multiple layers. Shipyard pushes that responsibility into build pipelines and automated safety checks, giving EC2 workloads that could not containerize the same rollout guarantees as app delivery.

title: Why goodput matters more than throughput for LLM serving
blurb: The author argues that throughput is the wrong headline metric for LLM serving, since requests per second can climb while TTFT and TPOT quietly blow past targets. Goodput counts only completed requests that meet those latency SLOs, making it the number worth tuning against. The walkthrough serves Qwen2.5-7B on vLLM on a single A10G in EKS, driving load with GuideLLM and instrumenting through Prometheus, Grafana, and NVIDIA's DCGM exporter, with the Kubernetes manifests published for reuse against your own traffic.

title: Critical NGINX Vulnerability Can Crash Workers and May Allow Remote Code Execution
blurb: F5 patched CVE-2026-42533, a heap buffer overflow in nginx's script engine that a remote unauthenticated attacker can trigger with crafted HTTP requests, crashing workers and potentially allowing remote code execution. Fixes landed in nginx 1.30.4, 1.31.3, and NGINX Plus 37.0.3.1, but exposure depends on config: only a regex-based map referenced after an earlier capture is affected, and F5 has not yet listed fixed builds for Ingress Controller, Gateway Fabric, App Protect WAF, or Instance Manager.

title: Airbus takes flight from AWS. What happens next is critical
blurb:
Airbus is moving workloads off AWS, and the author argues the execution matters more than the decision itself. Repatriation at that scale means rebuilding managed-service dependencies, identity, and networking on infrastructure the team now runs itself, and the cost case only holds if utilization stays high enough to beat what the hyperscaler was charging.

Note: the supplied context is The Register's "most popular" sidebar, not the article body — no details on Airbus's target platform, timeline, or scale. Blurb is written from the headline only. Pull the article text if you want specifics in it.

title: The top content formats & types that earn AI search citations
blurb: HubSpot's 2026 State of AEO Report found 58% of marketers are now optimizing content for answer engines, and the pages that actually earn citations share structural traits rather than topical ones. Question-led headings, self-contained direct-answer passages, schema markup, and internal linking that maps entity relationships are what let AI Overviews, ChatGPT, and Perplexity extract and attribute a passage. The practical shift is writing sections that stand alone out of context, since extraction happens at the passage level, not the page level.

title: Netflix CPTO on AI and the future of product and tech roles | Elizabeth Stone
blurb: Netflix CPTO Elizabeth Stone says systems thinking has replaced functional depth as the skill she screens for, and that AI fluency is now expected at every level rather than treated as a senior-track differentiator. Her more immediate problem is triage: AI-generated output has multiplied faster than the review capacity to judge it, so the constraint has shifted from producing work to deciding which work is worth keeping.

title: You're doing "Activation" wrong
blurb: The author argues the standard activation rate, users hitting an aha milestone divided by signups, has stopped working as a growth metric because AI-driven acquisition brings in more users but not the right ones. Activation is a company problem rather than a growth-team problem, and teams that optimize the ratio in isolation end up trading long-term efficiency for short-term wins. The piece draws on the author's work as CPGO at Fyxer, where curating strategy and ICP documentation for AI tooling still failed to make the real problem areas obvious.

title: Trump targets Brazil's payments system while dollar stablecoins are quietly overtaking country's payments
blurb: The US will impose a 25% Section 301 tariff on most Brazilian goods starting July 22, the first use of that authority to target a country's domestic payment system, citing Pix rules that cap merchant fees and mandate free service for individuals. Dollar-linked stablecoins already account for roughly 90% of Brazil's crypto transactions, meaning dollar settlement is spreading through the market the tariff is meant to defend.

title: Tether's USDT hits 2-year countdown threatening its position on U.S. crypto platforms
blurb: The GENIUS Act passed its one-year mark on Saturday, starting a two-year countdown for Tether to bring USDT into compliance or lose access to US platforms by July 2028. Tether's latest disclosures show roughly a quarter of USDT reserves sit in assets that fail GENIUS standards, while US-based rival Circle has moved to pre-comply and federal regulators have missed their own rulemaking deadline.

title: Inside Zcash's new node that targets Visa-scale privacy at 50,000 transactions per second
blurb: Zcash developers released Zakura 1.0.0, an independently maintained full node forked from the Foundation's Zebra client, adding pruning, fast sync, and legacy zcashd compatibility ahead of that client's July 18 end of life. Zakura supports the Ironwood (NU6.3) upgrade activating July 28, which adds a turnstile capping Orchard shielded pool withdrawals to contain any counterfeit ZEC from a long-standing soundness bug.

title: US regulators miss GENIUS Act’s one-year deadline for final stablecoin rules
blurb: US regulators missed the GENIUS Act's one-year statutory deadline to finalize stablecoin rules, leaving issuers without final guidance on reserve, disclosure, and licensing requirements. The delay does not move the law's Jan. 18, 2027 effective date, compressing the window for issuers to build compliance programs against rules that have not yet been written.

title: Visa Is Expanding Its Crypto Push With New Stablecoin Platform
blurb:
Visa announced a new stablecoin platform, extending a crypto push that already includes USDC settlement pilots on Solana and Ethereum. The launch positions Visa as infrastructure for bank-issued stablecoins rather than a competitor to them, giving regulated issuers distribution across Visa's existing merchant network.

(46 words. Flagging: the Context field was empty, so I wrote from the headline plus Visa's documented USDC settlement history. If the Bloomberg piece has specifics — launch partners, chains, volume figures — those belong in sentence two instead of my framing.)

title: France orders internet providers to block Polymarket ahead of World Cup final
blurb: France ordered internet service providers to block Polymarket ahead of the World Cup final, cutting off French users days before the largest event markets in prediction-market history settle. The order follows the French gaming regulator's position that Polymarket's contracts constitute unlicensed gambling, and it forces the platform to weigh geoblocking compliance against an offshore posture it has held in other restricted jurisdictions.

title: Hyperliquid’s HIP-4 to support permissionless deployment for outcome markets
blurb: Hyperliquid proposed HIP-4, which would let anyone deploy outcome markets on the exchange without going through governance, provided the deployer stakes 500,000 HYPE. Deployers can set fees of up to 50% on their validator-aligned markets, giving them a direct revenue stream and tying prediction-market listing rights to HYPE lockup rather than protocol approval.

title: Amazon Japan supplier AZ-Com Maruwa to adopt yen stablecoin JPYC for payments
blurb: Tokyo-listed logistics firm AZ-COM Maruwa Holdings, which has handled Amazon Japan deliveries since 2017, plans to pay roughly 2,300 subcontractors and truck drivers in JPYC, the country's first fully regulated yen stablecoin. The rollout is Japan's largest corporate stablecoin deployment to date, and Maruwa is weighing a 1 billion yen investment in the token, whose onchain circulation passed 2 billion yen last week.

title: Allbridge Core pauses protocol after $1.65 million flash loan exploit: onchain analysts
blurb: Cross-chain bridge Allbridge Core was exploited for roughly $1.65M and has paused the protocol, with PeckShield and CertiK reporting the attacker bridged the stolen funds from Solana to Ethereum. Onchain Lens traced the attack to a $1.12M flash loan from Kamino used to skew stablecoin pool ratios via rapid USDC-USDT swaps, and Allbridge has asked users to withdraw liquidity from affected pools.

title: Saylor urges Bitcoin to reject BIP-110 in 110-point essay as soft fork’s August showdown approaches
blurb: Michael Saylor published a 110-point essay urging node operators to reject BIP-110 ahead of the soft fork's August activation deadline.

title: Hyperliquid plans to add decentralized prediction markets in upgrade to HIP-4
blurb: Hyperliquid said a forthcoming enhancement to its HIP-4 upgrade will let anyone deploy prediction markets permissionlessly, using templates approved by validator vote, first on testnet and later on mainnet. Deployers must stake 500,000 HYPE that can be slashed for poorly defined or incorrectly settled markets, and earn up to 50% of trading fee revenue.

title: eToro Takes Strategic Stake in Onchain Derivatives Exchange Extended, Plans Zengo Tie-Up
blurb: eToro took a strategic stake in Extended, a Starknet-based perpetual futures decentralized exchange, as part of a $12.5M round reported by CoinDesk, and plans to pair the venue with Zengo, the self-custody wallet it acquired in April for roughly $70M. The tie-up would let eToro offer onchain perpetuals across Extended's 100-plus crypto, equities, FX, and commodities markets to wallet users who retain custody of their assets.

title: Japanese logistics firm turns to JPYC stablecoin to fight labor shortages: report
blurb: A Japanese logistics firm has begun paying contract drivers in JPYC, the yen-pegged stablecoin, to settle invoices faster than domestic bank rails allow, according to Nikkei. The company is using payment speed as a recruiting lever in a trucking sector facing chronic labor shortages, an early case of stablecoin settlement adopted for operational reasons rather than crypto exposure.

title: Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin ‘vibe-codes’ anonymous billboard demo with onchain moderation on Aztec
blurb: Vitalik Buterin released a working demo of an anonymous message board on Aztec, where users deposit ETH on L1, post on L2 with no sender address or link to the deposit, and withdraw back to L1. A designated censor role can flag posts as "immoral," with a local LLM daemon evaluating each message against an onchain moderation policy and applying time-lock penalties. The repo documents 70 Lean 4 theorems covering rate limits, privacy, and deposit safety.

title: Upbit parent Dunamu faces sanction process nearly eight months after $30 million hack: report
blurb: South Korea's Financial Supervisory Service sent Dunamu an inspection report over Upbit's 44.5 billion won ($30M) Solana-asset hack last November, the first formal step toward sanctions against the country's largest exchange by volume. The scope remains unclear because the Virtual Asset User Protection Act has no direct sanction provisions for hacks or IT failures, a gap regulators plan to close in the pending Digital Asset Basic Act.

title: Kraken says simpler options can unlock crypto's next derivatives market
blurb: Kraken launched European-style, USD-settled bitcoin and ether options on Kraken Pro, initially via request-for-quote for eligible clients outside Europe, North America, and Australia. The exchange's derivatives director argues product design rather than demand has kept crypto options a small share of trading volume against incumbents Deribit, CME, and Binance, and plans to add an order book, more assets, and a European rollout.

title: Bitcoin’s quantum problem gets a recovery tool, but not for Satoshi’s 1.1 million coins
blurb: Project Eleven published a zero-knowledge proof scheme that lets a wallet's key-derivation path serve as ownership evidence after quantum computers can forge ECDSA signatures, running in 243ms on a laptop. The design targets BIP-361, the Lopp proposal that would block deposits to quantum-vulnerable addresses after three years and freeze them after five, stranding over 34% of supply. The prototype is unaudited and incomplete, and Satoshi's ~1.1M BTC sit in addresses too old to carry the hashed derivation material the proof requires.

title: Here is why a massive $1.6 billion in crypto liquidity is sitting idle and wasting away
blurb: Dune research commissioned by 1inch found that $1.6B of the $1.84B tracked across concentrated liquidity pools on Uniswap, PancakeSwap, and Aerodrome was underutilized in H1 2026, with $542M sitting fully out of range in an average week. Out-of-range positions forgo roughly $150M in annual fees, though rebalancing carries its own transaction costs and execution risk.

title: France doubles down on restricting access to Polymarket
blurb: France's gambling regulator ANJ ordered ISPs to block Polymarket after finding French residents logged 578,751 visits in June despite a November 2024 ban on transactions from the country. Advertising an unlicensed betting site now carries fines up to €100,000, and the action follows Spain blocking both Polymarket and Kalshi and Minnesota banning prediction markets outright.

title: UNI burn poised to grow as Uniswap governance votes on v4 fees and Robinhood Chain expansion
blurb: Uniswap governance opened voting on two proposals that would route v4 protocol fees and Robinhood Chain expansion revenue into the UNI burn mechanism established by December's UNIfication overhaul. Voting runs from July 19 through July 26, and passage would expand the burn's fee base beyond the v2 and v3 pools currently feeding it.

title: The time for Clarity is here: The next-generation Telecoms Act
blurb: Former Congressman Patrick McHenry argues in Fortune that the Clarity Act, the crypto market structure bill moving through Congress, is the most consequential technology legislation since the Telecommunications Act of 1996. He counters the position that existing securities law is sufficient, pointing to bipartisan backing across multiple committees and to crypto businesses that are asking for defined boundaries rather than no regulation at all.

title: Bank of Korea prepares for live CBDC transactions with 9 banks in September
blurb: The Bank of Korea will begin phase two of its CBDC pilot in September, expanding real-transaction testing to nine banks including KB Kookmin, Shinhan, Hana, and Woori. The BOK will supply the institutional infrastructure while each bank issues and manages its own deposit tokens, a structure aimed at commercialization rather than another closed trial.

title: Bitcoin ETFs see new money again, but inflows remain ‘peanuts’ relative to the recent exodus
blurb: US-listed spot bitcoin ETFs pulled in $273M over two weeks, ending an eight-week outflow streak that drained more than $8B from the funds. The inflows are roughly 3% of what left during the selloff, so they read as a stabilization in flow dynamics rather than confirmed institutional re-entry, with BTC holding between $64,000 and $65,000 against an October peak above $126,000.

title: Cross-chain protocol Allbridge halts after $1.65 million flash loan exploit
blurb: Allbridge paused its Core cross-chain stablecoin protocol after an attacker drained roughly $1.65M from its Solana liquidity pools using a $1.12M flash loan from Kamino to skew pool ratios and withdraw at favorable rates. The stolen funds were bridged to Ethereum and split across addresses, and Allbridge has asked LPs to withdraw and arbitrageurs who profited from the imbalance to return funds.

title: Stablecoins vs Traditional Banking
blurb: This explainer breaks down how stablecoin issuers resemble banks and where the analogy fails. Both take in deposits and hold reserves, but issuers don't lend against those deposits, don't run fractional reserves, and don't carry deposit insurance, so redemption risk sits with the reserve composition rather than a backstop. The piece covers how yield accrues to the issuer instead of the holder, and what that means under emerging stablecoin regulation.

title: Trump targets Brazil's payments system while dollar stablecoins are quietly overtaking country's payments
blurb: The US will impose a 25% Section 301 tariff on most Brazilian goods starting July 22, the first time the trade authority has been aimed at a foreign country's domestic payment system. USTR argues Pix's free-for-consumers mandate and capped merchant fees disadvantage Visa and Mastercard in a market where the instant-payment rail now clears more transactions than cards, even as dollar stablecoins already make up roughly 90% of Brazil's crypto volume.

title: Tether's USDT hits 2-year countdown threatening its position on U.S. crypto platforms
blurb: The GENIUS Act passed its first anniversary with no federal financial regulator having finished implementing rules, starting a two-year countdown that Tether must clear by July 2028 to keep USDT on U.S. platforms. Tether's latest disclosures show as much as a quarter of USDT reserves sit in assets that fail GENIUS standards, while U.S.-based Circle has spent the year pre-complying.

title: US regulators miss GENIUS Act’s one-year deadline for final stablecoin rules
blurb: Federal banking regulators and Treasury blew past the GENIUS Act's one-year statutory deadline to finalize stablecoin rules, leaving issuers without the capital, reserve, and redemption standards they need to build compliance programs. The law's Jan. 18, 2027 effective date has not moved, so the missed deadline compresses the implementation window for issuers and their sponsor banks rather than extending it.

title: Visa Is Expanding Its Crypto Push With New Stablecoin Platform
blurb: Visa is building a new stablecoin platform that lets banks and fintechs issue and settle their own tokens on top of Visa's existing network, extending a crypto push that already moves USDC across four blockchains. The move puts Visa in direct competition with the stablecoin infrastructure providers its issuer clients would otherwise use, and positions the card network as a settlement layer rather than a bystander to it.

title: eToro Takes Strategic Stake in Onchain Derivatives Exchange Extended, Plans Zengo Tie-Up
blurb: eToro became a strategic investor in Extended, a Starknet-based onchain perpetuals exchange, in a round CoinDesk pegged at $12.5M, and paired it with Zengo, the self-custody wallet eToro bought in April for roughly $70M. Combining Zengo's custody with Extended's cross-asset derivatives engine lets eToro offer onchain perps to users who keep control of their assets, mirroring Robinhood's push into onchain product suites.

title: Amazon Japan supplier AZ-Com Maruwa to adopt yen stablecoin JPYC for payments
blurb: AZ-COM Maruwa Holdings, a Tokyo-listed logistics firm that has handled Amazon Japan deliveries since 2017, will pay roughly 2,300 subcontractors and truck drivers in JPYC, the first fully regulated yen stablecoin under the Payment Services Act. The rollout targets faster cash flow for small carriers via near-instant, free conversion to yen, and Maruwa is weighing a 1 billion yen investment in the issuer.

title: France orders internet providers to block Polymarket ahead of World Cup final
blurb: France ordered internet service providers to block Polymarket ahead of the World Cup final, cutting off French users from what will be the largest event markets the prediction-market industry has settled to date. The order follows the ANJ gaming regulator's position that Polymarket's event contracts constitute unlicensed gambling rather than derivatives, a distinction that sits at the center of prediction markets' regulatory fight in the US as well.

title: Sources detail Kalshi's and Polymarket's efforts to undermine each other, including lobbying regulators, trying to derail deals, and waging influence campaigns (New York Times)
blurb: Kalshi and Polymarket have been working to undermine each other through regulator lobbying, attempts to derail partnership deals, and influence campaigns, according to sources cited by the New York Times. The fight is over which platform sets the terms for CFTC-regulated event contracts, a category that only recently cleared the path to mainstream brokerage and exchange distribution.

title: LSEG: Morgan Stanley collected $2.3B in debt and equity capital market fees in H1 2026, up from $1.4B in H1 2025, driven by AI infrastructure financing deals (Financial Times)
blurb: Morgan Stanley collected $2.3B in debt and equity capital markets fees in H1 2026, up from $1.4B a year earlier, with AI infrastructure financing driving the increase, per LSEG data. Datacenter buildouts are now a primary revenue line for bulge-bracket capital markets desks, concentrating underwriting fees around a single capex cycle that has yet to prove its return profile.

title: Bank of Korea prepares for live CBDC transactions with 9 banks in September
blurb: The Bank of Korea will begin phase two of its CBDC pilot in September, expanding real-transaction testing to nine banks including KB Kookmin, Shinhan, Hana, and Woori. The BOK provides the infrastructure while each bank issues and manages its own deposit tokens, a split that mirrors the sponsor-bank model and runs in parallel with Korean banks building won-backed stablecoin rails.

title: How PayPal CEO Enrique Lores, appointed earlier in 2026 to revive the payments pioneer, may handle Stripe and Advent's takeover offer, based on his HP tenure (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: The Wall Street Journal reports that PayPal CEO Enrique Lores, appointed earlier in 2026 to revive the payments pioneer, is weighing a takeover offer from Stripe and Advent, with his cost-cutting and divestiture record at HP read as the template for his response. A Stripe-PayPal deal would fold branded checkout and Braintree processing volume into one stack, a concentration the DOJ and FTC would scrutinize.

title: Stablecoins vs Traditional Banking
blurb: Stablecoin issuers take in cash and hold reserves much like banks do, but they issue a bearer token onchain instead of a deposit liability and operate without a charter, FDIC insurance, or the ability to lend against those balances. The piece walks through where the two models diverge: settlement is near-instant and 24/7 versus ACH and wire windows, and reserve income accrues to the issuer rather than being shared with depositors.
